```
1	---
2	id: infinite-queries
3	title: Infinite Queries
4	sidebar_label: Infinite Queries
5	hide_title: true
6	description: 'RTK Query > Usage > Infinite Queries: fetching many data pages from a server'
7	---
8	
9	&nbsp;
10	
11	# Infinite Queries
12	
13	## Overview
14	
15	Rendering lists that can additively "load more" data onto an existing set of data or "infinite scroll" is a common UI pattern.
16	
17	RTK Query supports this use case via "infinite query" endpoints. Infinite Query endpoints are similar to standard query endpoints, in that they fetch data and cache the results. However, infinite query endpoints have the ability to fetch "next" and "previous" pages, and contain all related fetched pages in a single cache entry.
18	
19	## Infinite Query Concepts
20	
21	RTK Query's support for infinite queries is modeled after [React Query's infinite query API design](https://tanstack.com/query/latest/docs/framework/react/guides/infinite-queries).
22	
23	### Query Args, Page Params, and Cache Structure
24	
25	With standard query endpoints:
26	
27	- You specify the "query arg" value, which is passed to the `query` or `queryFn` function that will calculate the desired URL or do the actual fetching
28	- The query arg is also serialized to generate the unique internal key for this specific cache entry
29	- The single response value is directly stored as the `data` field in the cache entry
30	
31	Infinite queries work similarly, but have a couple additional layers:
32	
33	- You still specify a "query arg", which is still used to generate the unique cache key for this specific cache entry
34	- However, there is a separation between the "query arg" used for the cache key, and the "page param" used to fetch a specific page. Since both are useful for determining what to fetch, **your `query` and `queryFn` methods will receive a combined object with `{queryArg, pageParam}` as the first argument, instead of just the `queryArg` by itself**.
35	- The `data` field in the cache entry stores a `{pages: DataType[], pageParams: PageParam[]}` structure that contains _all_ of the fetched page results and their corresponding page params used to fetch them.
36	
37	For example, a Pokemon API endpoint might have a string query arg like `"fire"`, but use a page number as the param to determine which page to fetch out of the results. For a query like `useGetPokemonInfiniteQuery('fire')`, the resulting cache data might look like this:
38	
39	```ts no-transpile
40	{
41	  queries: {
42	    "getPokemon('fire')": {
43	      data: {
44	        pages: [
45	          ["Charmander", "Charmeleon"],
46	          ["Charizard", "Vulpix"],
47	          ["Magmar", "Flareon"]
48	        ],
49	        pageParams: [
50	          1,
51	          2,
52	          3
53	        ]
54	      }
55	    }
56	  }
57	}
58	```
59	
60	This structure allows flexibility in how your UI chooses to render the data (showing individual pages, flattening into a single list), enables limiting how many pages are kept in cache, and makes it possible to dynamically determine the next or previous page to fetch based on either the data or the page params.
61	
62	## Defining Infinite Query Endpoints
63	
64	Infinite query endpoints are defined by returning an object inside the `endpoints` section of `createApi`, and defining the fields using the `build.infiniteQuery()` method. They are an extension of standard query endpoints - you can specify [the same options as standard queries](./queries.mdx#defining-query-endpoints) (providing either `query` or `queryFn`, customizing with `transformResponse`, lifecycles with `onCacheEntryAdded` and `onQueryStarted`, defining tags, etc). However, they also require an additional `infiniteQueryOptions` field to specify the infinite query behavior.
65	
66	With TypeScript, you must supply 3 generic arguments: `build.infiniteQuery<ResultType, QueryArg, PageParam>`, where `ResultType` is the contents of a single page, `QueryArg` is the type passed in as the cache key, and `PageParam` is the value used to request a specific page. If there is no argument, use `void` for the arg type instead.
67	
68	### `infiniteQueryOptions`
69	
70	The `infiniteQueryOptions` field includes:
71	
72	- `initialPageParam`: the default page param value used for the first request, if this was not specified at the usage site
73	- `maxPages`: an optional limit to how many fetched pages will be kept in the cache entry at a time
74	- `getNextPageParam`: a required callback you must provide to calculate the next page param, given the existing cached pages and page params
75	- `getPreviousPageParam`: an optional callback that will be used to calculate the previous page param, if you try to fetch backwards.
76	
77	Both `initialPageParam` and `getNextPageParam` are required, to
78	ensure the infinite query can properly fetch the next page of data. Also, `initialPageParam` may be specified when using the endpoint, to override the default value for a first fetch. `maxPages` and `getPreviousPageParam` are both optional.
79	
80	### Page Param Functions
81	
82	`getNextPageParam` and `getPreviousPageParam` are user-defined, giving you flexibility to determine how those values are calculated:
83	
84	```ts
85	export type PageParamFunction<DataType, PageParam, QueryArg> = (
86	  currentPage: DataType,
87	  allPages: DataType[],
88	  currentPageParam: PageParam,
89	  allPageParams: PageParam[],
90	  queryArg: QueryArg,
91	) => PageParam | undefined | null
92	```
93	
94	A page param can be any value at all: numbers, strings, objects, arrays, etc. Since the existing page param values are stored in Redux state, you should still treat those immutably. For example, if you had a param structure like `{page: Number, filters: Filters}`, incrementing the page would look like `return {...currentPageParam, page: currentPageParam.page + 1}`.
95	
96	Since both actual page contents and page params are passed in, you can calculate new page params based on any of those. This enables a number of possible infinite query use cases, including cursor-based and limit+offset-based queries.
97	
98	The "current" arguments will be either the last page for `getNextPageParam`, or the first page for `getPreviousPageParam`.
99	
100	The list of arguments is the same as with React Query, but with the addition of `queryArg` at the end. (This is because React Query always has access to the query arg when you pass the options to its `useQuery` hook, but with RTK Query the endpoints are defined separately, so this makes the query arg accessible if you need it to calculate the page params.)
101	
102	If there is no possible page to fetch in that direction, the callback should return `undefined`.
103	
104	### Infinite Query Definition Example
105	
106	A complete example of this for a fictional Pokemon API service might look like:
107	
108	```ts no-transpile title="Infinite Query definition example"
109	type Pokemon = {
110	  id: string
111	  name: string
112	}
113	
114	const pokemonApi = createApi({
115	  baseQuery: fetchBaseQuery({ baseUrl: 'https://example.com/pokemon' }),
116	  endpoints: (build) => ({
117	    // 3 TS generics: page contents, query arg, page param
118	    getInfinitePokemonWithMax: build.infiniteQuery<Pokemon[], string, number>({
119	      infiniteQueryOptions: {
120	        // Must provide a default initial page param value
121	        initialPageParam: 1,
122	        // Optionally limit the number of cached pages
123	        maxPages: 3,
124	        // Must provide a `getNextPageParam` function
125	        getNextPageParam: (
126	          lastPage,
127	          allPages,
128	          lastPageParam,
129	          allPageParams,
130	          queryArg,
131	        ) => lastPageParam + 1,
132	        // Optionally provide a `getPreviousPageParam` function
133	        getPreviousPageParam: (
134	          firstPage,
135	          allPages,
136	          firstPageParam,
137	          allPageParams,
138	          queryArg,
139	        ) => {
140	          return firstPageParam > 0 ? firstPageParam - 1 : undefined
141	        },
142	      },
143	      // The `query` function receives `{queryArg, pageParam}` as its argument
144	      query({ queryArg, pageParam }) {
145	        return `/type/${queryArg}?page=${pageParam}`
146	      },
147	    }),
148	  }),
149	})
150	```
151	
152	## Performing Infinite Queries with React Hooks
153	
154	[Similar to query endpoints](./queries.mdx#performing-queries-with-react-hooks), RTK Query will automatically generate React hooks for infinite query endpoints based on the name of the endpoint. An endpoint field with `getPokemon: build.infiniteQuery()` will generate a hook named `useGetPokemonInfiniteQuery`, as well as a generically-named hook attached to the endpoint, like `api.endpoints.getPokemon.useInfiniteQuery`.
155	
156	### Hook Types
157	
158	There are 3 infinite query-related hooks:
159	
160	1. [`useInfiniteQuery`](../api/created-api/hooks.mdx#useinfinitequery)
161	   - Composes `useInfiniteQuerySubscription` and `useInfiniteQueryState`, and is the primary hook. Automatically triggers fetches of data from an endpoint, 'subscribes' the component to the cached data, and reads the request status and cached data from the Redux store.
162	2. [`useInfiniteQuerySubscription`](../api/created-api/hooks.mdx#useinfinitequerysubscription)
163	   - Returns a `refetch` function and `fetchNext/PreviousPage` functions, and accepts all hooks options. Automatically triggers refetches of data from an endpoint, and 'subscribes' the component to the cached data.
164	3. [`useInfiniteQueryState`](../api/created-api/hooks.mdx#useinfinitequerystate)
165	   - Returns the query state and accepts `skip` and `selectFromResult`. Reads the request status and cached data from the Redux store.
166	
167	In practice, the standard `useInfiniteQuery`-based hooks such as `useGetPokemonInfiniteQuery` will be the primary hooks used in your application, but the other hooks are available for specific use cases.
168	
169	### Query Hook Options
170	
171	The query hooks expect two parameters: `(queryArg?, queryOptions?)`.
172	
173	The `queryOptions` object accepts [all the same parameters as `useQuery`](./queries.mdx#query-hook-options), including `skip`, `selectFromResult`, and refetching/polling options.
174	
175	Unlike normal query hooks, your `query` or `queryFn` callbacks will receive a "page param" value to generate the URL or make the request, instead of the "query arg" that was passed to the hook. By default, the `initialPageParam` value specified in the endpoint will be used to make the first request, and then your `getNext/PreviousPageParam` callbacks will be used to calculate further page params as you fetch forwards or backwards.
176	
177	If you want to start from a different page param, you may override the `initialPageParam` by passing it as part of the hook options:
178	
179	```ts no-transpile
180	const { data } = useGetPokemonInfiniteQuery('fire', {
181	  initialPageParam: 3,
182	})
183	```
184	
185	The next and previous page params will still be calculated as needed.
186	
187	### Frequently Used Query Hook Return Values
188	
189	Infinite query hooks return [the same result object as normal query hooks](./queries.mdx#frequently-used-query-hook-return-values), but with [a few additional fields specific to infinite queries](../api/created-api/hooks.mdx#useinfinitequery-signature) and a different structure for `data` and `currentData`.
190	
191	- `data` / `currentData`: These contain the same "latest successful" and "latest for current arg" results as normal queries, but the value is the `{pages, pageParams}` infinite query object with all fetched pages instead of a single response value.
192	- `hasNextPage` / `hasPreviousPage`: When true, indicates that there _should_ be another page available to fetch in that direction. This is calculated by calling `getNext/PreviousPageParam` with the latest fetched pages.
193	- `isFetchingNext/PreviousPage`: When true, indicates that the current `isFetching` flag represents a fetch in that direction.
194	- `isFetchNext/PreviousPageError`: When true, indicates that the current `isError` flag represents an error for a failed fetch in that direction
195	- `fetchNext/PreviousPage`: methods that will trigger a fetch for another page in that direction.
196	
197	In most cases, you will probably read `data` and either `isLoading` or `isFetching` in order to render your UI. You will also want to use the `fetchNext/PreviousPage` methods to trigger fetching additional pages.
198	
199	### Displaying Infinite Query Data
200	
201	For infinite query hooks, the `data` field returned by the hook will be the `{pages, pageParams}` structure containing all fetched pages, instead of just a single response value.
202	
203	This gives you control over how the data is used for display. You can flatten all the page contents into a single array for infinite scrolling, use the individual page results for pagination, sort or reverse the entries, or any other logic you need for rendering the UI with this data.
204	
205	As with any other Redux data, you should avoid mutating these arrays (including calling `array.sort/reverse()` directly on the existing references).
206	
207	### Infinite Query Hook Usage Example
208	
209	Here is an example of a typical infinite query endpoint definition, and hook usage in a component:
210	
211	```tsx no-transpile
212	type Pokemon = {
213	  id: string
214	  name: string
215	}
216	
217	const pokemonApi = createApi({
218	  baseQuery: fetchBaseQuery({ baseUrl: 'https://example.com/pokemon' }),
219	  endpoints: (build) => ({
220	    getPokemon: build.infiniteQuery<Pokemon[], string, number>({
221	      infiniteQueryOptions: {
222	        initialPageParam: 1,
223	        getNextPageParam: (lastPage, allPages, lastPageParam, allPageParams) =>
224	          lastPageParam + 1,
225	      },
226	      query({ queryArg, pageParam }) {
227	        return `/type/${queryArg}?page=${pageParam}`
228	      },
229	    }),
230	  }),
231	})
232	
233	function PokemonList({ pokemonType }: { pokemonType: string }) {
234	  const { data, isFetching, fetchNextPage, fetchPreviousPage, refetch } =
235	    pokemonApi.useGetPokemonInfiniteQuery(pokemonType)
236	
237	  const handleNextPage = async () => {
238	    await fetchNextPage()
239	  }
240	
241	  const handleRefetch = async () => {
242	    await refetch()
243	  }
244	
245	  const allResults = data?.pages.flat() ?? []
246	
247	  return (
248	    <div>
249	      <div>Type: {pokemonType}</div>
250	      <div>
251	        {allResults.map((pokemon, i: number | null | undefined) => (
252	          <div key={i}>{pokemon.name}</div>
253	        ))}
254	      </div>
255	      <button onClick={() => handleNextPage()}>Fetch More</button>
256	      <button onClick={() => handleRefetch()}>Refetch</button>
257	    </div>
258	  )
259	}
260	```
261	
262	In this example, the server returns an array of Pokemon as the response for each individual page. This component shows the results as a single list. Since the `data` field itself has a `pages` array of all responses, the component needs to flatten the pages into a single array to render that list. Alternately, it could map over the pages and show them in a paginated format.
263	
264	Similarly, this example relies on manual user clicks on a "Fetch More" button to trigger fetching the next page, but could automatically call `fetchNextPage` based on things like an `IntersectionObserver`, a list component triggering some kind of "end of the list" event, or other similar indicators.
265	
266	The endpoint itself only defines `getNextPageParam`, so this example doesn't support fetching backwards, but that can be provided in cases where backwards fetching makes sense. The page param here is a simple incremented number, but the page param
267	
268	## Infinite Query Behaviors
269	
270	### Overlapping Page Fetches
271	
272	Since all pages are stored in a single cache entry, there can only be one request in progress at a time. RTK Query already has logic built in to bail out of running a new request if there is already a request in flight for that cache entry.
273	
274	That means that if you call `fetchNextPage()` again while an existing request is in progress, the second call won't actually execute a request. Be sure to either await the previous `fetchNextPage()` promise result first or check the `isFetching` flag if you have concerns about a potential request already in progress.
275	
276	The promise returned from `fetchNextPage()` does have [a `promise.abort()` method attached](../../api/createAsyncThunk.mdx#canceling-while-running) that will force the earlier request to reject and not save the results. Note that this will mark the cache entry as errored, but the data will still exist. Since `promise.abort()` is synchronous, you would also need to await the previous promise to ensure the rejection is handled, and then trigger the new page fetch.
277	
278	### Refetching
279	
280	When an infinite query endpoint is refetched (due to tag invalidation, polling, arg change configuration, or manual refetching), RTK Query's default behavior is **sequentially refetching _all_ pages currently in the cache**. This ensures that the client is always working with the latest data, and avoids stale cursors or duplicate records.
281	
282	If the cache entry is ever removed and then re-added, it will start with only fetching the initial page.
283	
284	There may be cases when you want a refetch to _only_ refetch the first page, and not any of the other pages in cache (ie, the refetch shrinks the cache from N pages to 1 page). This can be done via the `refetchCachedPages` option, which can be passed in several different places:
285	
286	- Defined on the endpoint as part of `infiniteQueryOptions`: applies to all attempted refetches
287	- Passed as an option to a `useInfiniteQuery` hook: applies to all attempted refetches
288	- Passed as an option to `endpoint.initiate()`, or the `refetch` method available on the `initiate` or hook result objects: applies only to the manually triggered refetch
289	
290	Overall, the refetch logic defaults to refetching all pages, but will override that with the option provided to the endpoint or a manual refetch.
291	
292	### Limiting Cache Entry Size
293	
294	All fetched pages for a given query arg are stored in the `pages` array in that cache entry. By default, there is no limit to the number of stored pages - if you call `fetchNextPage()` 1000 times, `data.pages` will have 1000 pages stored.
295	
296	If you need to limit the number of stored pages (for reasons like memory usage), you can supply a `maxPages` option as part of the endpoint. If provided, fetching a page when already at the max will automatically drop the last page in the opposite direction. For example, with `maxPages: 3` and a cached page params of `[1, 2, 3]`, calling `fetchNextPage()` would result in page `1` being dropped and the new cached pages being `[2, 3, 4]`. From there, calling `fetchNextPage()` would result in `[3, 4, 5]`, or calling `fetchPreviousPage()` would go back to `[1, 2, 3]`.
297	
298	## Common Infinite Query Patterns
299	
300	The `getNext/PreviousPageParam` callbacks offer flexibility in how you interact with the backend API.
```
````

1	import { afterEach, beforeEach, describe, expect, test, vi } from 'vitest'
2	import { queryKey, sleep } from '@tanstack/query-test-utils'
3	import { InfiniteQueryObserver, QueryClient } from '..'
4	import type {
5	  DefaultedInfiniteQueryObserverOptions,
6	  InfiniteData,
7	} from '../types'
8	
9	describe('InfiniteQueryObserver', () => {
10	  let queryClient: QueryClient
11	
12	  beforeEach(() => {
13	    vi.useFakeTimers()
14	    queryClient = new QueryClient()
15	    queryClient.mount()
16	  })
17	
18	  afterEach(() => {
19	    queryClient.clear()
20	    vi.useRealTimers()
21	  })
22	
23	  test('should be able to fetch an infinite query with selector', async () => {
24	    const key = queryKey()
25	    const observer = new InfiniteQueryObserver(queryClient, {
26	      queryKey: key,
27	      queryFn: () => sleep(10).then(() => 1),
28	      select: (data) => ({
29	        pages: data.pages.map((x) => `${x}`),
30	        pageParams: data.pageParams,
31	      }),
32	      initialPageParam: 1,
33	      getNextPageParam: () => 2,
34	    })
35	    let observerResult
36	    const unsubscribe = observer.subscribe((result) => {
37	      observerResult = result
38	    })
39	    await vi.advanceTimersByTimeAsync(10)
40	    unsubscribe()
41	    expect(observerResult).toMatchObject({
42	      data: { pages: ['1'], pageParams: [1] },
43	    })
44	  })
45	
46	  test('should pass the meta option to the queryFn', async () => {
47	    const meta = {
48	      it: 'works',
49	    }
50	
51	    const key = queryKey()
52	    const queryFn = vi.fn(() => sleep(10).then(() => 1))
53	    const observer = new InfiniteQueryObserver(queryClient, {
54	      meta,
55	      queryKey: key,
56	      queryFn,
57	      select: (data) => ({
58	        pages: data.pages.map((x) => `${x}`),
59	        pageParams: data.pageParams,
60	      }),
61	      initialPageParam: 1,
62	      getNextPageParam: () => 2,
63	    })
64	    let observerResult
65	    const unsubscribe = observer.subscribe((result) => {
66	      observerResult = result
67	    })
68	    await vi.advanceTimersByTimeAsync(10)
69	    unsubscribe()
70	    expect(observerResult).toMatchObject({
71	      data: { pages: ['1'], pageParams: [1] },
72	    })
73	    expect(queryFn).toBeCalledWith(expect.objectContaining({ meta }))
74	  })
75	
76	  test('should make getNextPageParam and getPreviousPageParam receive current pageParams', async () => {
77	    const key = queryKey()
78	    let single: Array<string> = []
79	    let all: Array<string> = []
80	    const observer = new InfiniteQueryObserver(queryClient, {
81	      queryKey: key,
82	      queryFn: ({ pageParam }) => sleep(10).then(() => String(pageParam)),
83	      initialPageParam: 1,
84	      getNextPageParam: (_, __, lastPageParam, allPageParams) => {
85	        single.push('next' + lastPageParam)
86	        all.push('next' + allPageParams.join(','))
87	        return lastPageParam + 1
88	      },
89	      getPreviousPageParam: (_, __, firstPageParam, allPageParams) => {
90	        single.push('prev' + firstPageParam)
91	        all.push('prev' + allPageParams.join(','))
92	        return firstPageParam - 1
93	      },
94	    })
95	    await vi.advanceTimersByTimeAsync(10)
96	
97	    observer.fetchNextPage()
98	    await vi.advanceTimersByTimeAsync(10)
99	    observer.fetchPreviousPage()
100	    await vi.advanceTimersByTimeAsync(10)
101	
102	    expect(single).toEqual(['next1', 'prev1', 'prev1', 'next1', 'prev0'])
103	    expect(all).toEqual(['next1', 'prev1', 'prev1', 'next0,1', 'prev0,1'])
104	
105	    single = []
106	    all = []
107	
108	    observer.refetch()
109	    await vi.advanceTimersByTimeAsync(20)
110	
111	    expect(single).toEqual(['next0', 'next1', 'prev0'])
112	    expect(all).toEqual(['next0', 'next0,1', 'prev0,1'])
113	  })
114	
115	  test('should not invoke getNextPageParam and getPreviousPageParam on empty pages', () => {
116	    const key = queryKey()
117	
118	    const getNextPageParam = vi.fn()
119	    const getPreviousPageParam = vi.fn()
120	
121	    const observer = new InfiniteQueryObserver(queryClient, {
122	      queryKey: key,
123	      queryFn: ({ pageParam }) => sleep(10).then(() => String(pageParam)),
124	      initialPageParam: 1,
125	      getNextPageParam: getNextPageParam.mockImplementation(
126	        (_, __, lastPageParam) => lastPageParam + 1,
127	      ),
128	      getPreviousPageParam: getPreviousPageParam.mockImplementation(
129	        (_, __, firstPageParam) => firstPageParam - 1,
130	      ),
131	    })
132	
133	    const unsubscribe = observer.subscribe(() => {})
134	
135	    getNextPageParam.mockClear()
136	    getPreviousPageParam.mockClear()
137	
138	    queryClient.setQueryData(key, { pages: [], pageParams: [] })
139	
140	    expect(getNextPageParam).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(0)
141	    expect(getPreviousPageParam).toHaveBeenCalledTimes(0)
142	
143	    unsubscribe()
144	  })
145	
146	  test('should stop refetching if undefined is returned from getNextPageParam', async () => {
147	    const key = queryKey()

234	    any,
235	    any
236	  >
237	    ? InfiniteQueryArgFrom<Definitions[EndpointName]>
238	    : Definitions[EndpointName] extends QueryDefinition<any, any, any, any>
239	      ? QueryArgFrom<Definitions[EndpointName]>
240	      : never
241	
242	export type DataFromAnyQueryDefinition<
243	  Definitions extends EndpointDefinitions,
244	  EndpointName extends AllQueryKeys<Definitions>,
245	> =
246	  Definitions[EndpointName] extends InfiniteQueryDefinition<
247	    any,
248	    any,
249	    any,
250	    any,
251	    any
252	  >
253	    ? InfiniteData<
254	        ResultTypeFrom<Definitions[EndpointName]>,
255	        PageParamFrom<Definitions[EndpointName]>
256	      >
257	    : Definitions[EndpointName] extends QueryDefinition<any, any, any, any>
258	      ? ResultTypeFrom<Definitions[EndpointName]>
259	      : unknown
260	
261	export type UpsertThunkResult<
262	  Definitions extends EndpointDefinitions,
263	  EndpointName extends AllQueryKeys<Definitions>,
264	> =
265	  Definitions[EndpointName] extends InfiniteQueryDefinition<
266	    any,
267	    any,
268	    any,
269	    any,
270	    any
271	  >
272	    ? InfiniteQueryActionCreatorResult<Definitions[EndpointName]>
273	    : Definitions[EndpointName] extends QueryDefinition<any, any, any, any>
274	      ? QueryActionCreatorResult<Definitions[EndpointName]>
275	      : QueryActionCreatorResult<never>
276	
277	export type UpdateQueryDataThunk<
278	  Definitions extends EndpointDefinitions,

281	  endpointName: EndpointName,

284	  updateProvided?: boolean,

286

  onSuccess?: (
  onError?: (
  onSettled?: (
  onSuccess?: (
  onError?: (
  onSettled?: (
   * @param options.onSuccess - This function will fire when the mutation is successful and will be passed the mutation's result.
   * @param options.onError - This function will fire if the mutation encounters an error and will be passed the error.
   * @param options.onSettled - This function will fire when the mutation is either successfully fetched or encounters an error and be passed either the data or error.
   * - If you make multiple requests, `onSuccess` will fire only after the latest call you've made.
   * - All the callback functions (`onSuccess`, `onError`, `onSettled`) are void functions, and the returned value will be ignored.
```

```
100	  build<
101	    TQueryFnData = unknown,
102	    TError = DefaultError,
103	    TData = TQueryFnData,
104	    TQueryKey extends QueryKey = QueryKey,
105	  >(
106	    client: QueryClient,
107	    options: WithRequired<
108	      QueryOptions<TQueryFnData, TError, TData, TQueryKey>,
109	      'queryKey'
110	    >,
111	    state?: QueryState<TData, TError>,
112	  ): Query<TQueryFnData, TError, TData, TQueryKey> {
113	    const queryKey = options.queryKey
114	    const queryHash =
115	      options.queryHash ?? hashQueryKeyByOptions(queryKey, options)
116	    let query = this.get<TQueryFnData, TError, TData, TQueryKey>(queryHash)
117	
118	    if (!query) {
119	      query = new Query({
120	        client,
121	        queryKey,
122	        queryHash,
123	        options: client.defaultQueryOptions(options),
124	        state,
125	        defaultOptions: client.getQueryDefaults(queryKey),
126	      })
127	      this.add(query)
128	    }
129	
130	    return query
131	  }
132	
133	  add(query: Query<any, any, any, any>): void {
134	    if (!this.#queries.has(query.queryHash)) {
135	      this.#queries.set(query.queryHash, query)
136	
137	      this.notify({
138	        type: 'added',
139	        query,
140	      })
141	    }
142	  }
143	
144	  remove(query: Query<any, any, any, any>): void {
145	    const queryInMap = this.#queries.get(query.queryHash)
146	
147	    if (queryInMap) {
148	      query.destroy()
149	
150	      if (queryInMap === query) {
151	        this.#queries.delete(query.queryHash)
152	      }
153	
154	      this.notify({ type: 'removed', query })
155	    }
156	  }
157	
158	  clear(): void {
159	    notifyManager.batch(() => {
160	      this.getAll().forEach((query) => {
161	        this.remove(query)
162	      })
163	    })
164	  }
165	
166	  get<
167	    TQueryFnData = unknown,
168	    TError = DefaultError,
169	    TData = TQueryFnData,
170	    TQueryKey extends QueryKey = QueryKey,
171	  >(
172	    queryHash: string,
173	  ): Query<TQueryFnData, TError, TData, TQueryKey> | undefined {
174	    return this.#queries.get(queryHash) as
175	      | Query<TQueryFnData, TError, TData, TQueryKey>
176	      | undefined
177	  }
178	
179	  getAll(): Array<Query> {
180	    return [...this.#queries.values()]
181	  }
182	
183	  find<TQueryFnData = unknown, TError = DefaultError, TData = TQueryFnData>(
184	    filters: WithRequired<QueryFilters, 'queryKey'>,
185	  ): Query<TQueryFnData, TError, TData> | undefined {
186	    const defaultedFilters = { exact: true, ...filters }
187	
188	    return this.getAll().find((query) =>
189	      matchQuery(defaultedFilters, query),
190	    ) as Query<TQueryFnData, TError, TData> | undefined
191	  }
192	
193	  findAll(filters: QueryFilters<any> = {}): Array<Query> {
194	    const queries = this.getAll()
195	    return Object.keys(filters).length > 0
196	      ? queries.filter((query) => matchQuery(filters, query))
197	      : queries
198	  }
199	
200	  notify(event: QueryCacheNotifyEvent): void {
201	    notifyManager.batch(() => {
202	      this.listeners.forEach((listener) => {
203	        listener(event)
204	      })
205	    })
206	  }
207	
208	  onFocus(): void {
209	    notifyManager.batch(() => {
210	      this.getAll().forEach((query) => {
211	        query.onFocus()
212	      })
213	    })
214	  }
215	
216	  onOnline(): void {
217	    notifyManager.batch(() => {
218	      this.getAll().forEach((query) => {
219	        query.onOnline()
220	      })
221	    })
222	  }
223	}
224	
```
````

```
1	import type { ThunkDispatch, UnknownAction } from '@reduxjs/toolkit'
2	import type {
3	  BaseQueryFn,
4	  BaseQueryMeta,
5	  BaseQueryResult,
6	} from '../../baseQueryTypes'
7	import type {
8	  BaseEndpointDefinition,
9	  DefinitionType,
10	} from '../../endpointDefinitions'
11	import { isAnyQueryDefinition } from '../../endpointDefinitions'
12	import type { QueryCacheKey, RootState } from '../apiState'
13	import type {
14	  MutationResultSelectorResult,
15	  QueryResultSelectorResult,
16	} from '../buildSelectors'
17	import { getMutationCacheKey } from '../buildSlice'
18	import type { PatchCollection, Recipe } from '../buildThunks'
19	import { isAsyncThunkAction, isFulfilled } from '../rtkImports'
20	import type {
21	  ApiMiddlewareInternalHandler,
22	  InternalHandlerBuilder,
23	  PromiseWithKnownReason,
24	  SubMiddlewareApi,
25	} from './types'
26	import { getEndpointDefinition } from '@internal/query/apiTypes'
27	
28	export type ReferenceCacheLifecycle = never
29	
30	export interface QueryBaseLifecycleApi<
31	  QueryArg,
32	  BaseQuery extends BaseQueryFn,
33	  ResultType,
34	  ReducerPath extends string = string,
35	> extends LifecycleApi<ReducerPath> {
36	  /**
37	   * Gets the current value of this cache entry.
38	   */
39	  getCacheEntry(): QueryResultSelectorResult<
40	    { type: DefinitionType.query } & BaseEndpointDefinition<
41	      QueryArg,
42	      BaseQuery,
43	      ResultType,
44	      BaseQueryResult<BaseQuery>
45	    >
46	  >
47	  /**
48	   * Updates the current cache entry value.
49	   * For documentation see `api.util.updateQueryData`.
50	   */
51	  updateCachedData(updateRecipe: Recipe<ResultType>): PatchCollection
52	}
53	
54	export type MutationBaseLifecycleApi<
55	  QueryArg,
56	  BaseQuery extends BaseQueryFn,
57	  ResultType,
58	  ReducerPath extends string = string,
59	> = LifecycleApi<ReducerPath> & {
60	  /**
61	   * Gets the current value of this cache entry.
62	   */
63	  getCacheEntry(): MutationResultSelectorResult<
64	    { type: DefinitionType.mutation } & BaseEndpointDefinition<
65	      QueryArg,
66	      BaseQuery,
67	      ResultType,
68	      BaseQueryResult<BaseQuery>
69	    >
70	  >
71	}
72	
73	type LifecycleApi<ReducerPath extends string = string> = {
74	  /**
75	   * The dispatch method for the store
76	   */
77	  dispatch: ThunkDispatch<any, any, UnknownAction>
78	  /**
79	   * A method to get the current state
80	   */
81	  getState(): RootState<any, any, ReducerPath>
82	  /**
83	   * `extra` as provided as `thunk.extraArgument` to the `configureStore` `getDefaultMiddleware` option.
84	   */
85	  extra: unknown
86	  /**
87	   * A unique ID generated for the mutation
88	   */
89	  requestId: string
90	}
91	
92	type CacheLifecyclePromises<ResultType = unknown, MetaType = unknown> = {
93	  /**
94	   * Promise that will resolve with the first value for this cache key.
95	   * This allows you to `await` until an actual value is in cache.
96	   *
97	   * If the cache entry is removed from the cache before any value has ever
98	   * been resolved, this Promise will reject with
99	   * `new Error('Promise never resolved before cacheEntryRemoved.')`
100	   * to prevent memory leaks.
101	   * You can just re-throw that error (or not handle it at all) -
102	   * it will be caught outside of `cacheEntryAdded`.
103	   *
104	   * If you don't interact with this promise, it will not throw.
105	   */
106	  cacheDataLoaded: PromiseWithKnownReason<
107	    {
108	      /**
109	       * The (transformed) query result.
110	       */
111	      data: ResultType
112	      /**
113	       * The `meta` returned by the `baseQuery`
114	       */
115	      meta: MetaType
116	    },
117	    typeof neverResolvedError
118	  >
119	  /**
120	   * Promise that allows you to wait for the point in time when the cache entry
121	   * has been removed from the cache, by not being used/subscribed to any more
122	   * in the application for too long or by dispatching `api.util.resetApiState`.
123	   */
124	  cacheEntryRemoved: Promise<void>
125	}
126	
127	export interface QueryCacheLifecycleApi<
128	  QueryArg,
129	  BaseQuery extends BaseQueryFn,
130	  ResultType,
131	  ReducerPath extends string = string,
132	> extends QueryBaseLifecycleApi<QueryArg, BaseQuery, ResultType, ReducerPath>,
133	    CacheLifecyclePromises<ResultType, BaseQueryMeta<BaseQuery>> {}
134	
135	export type MutationCacheLifecycleApi<
136	  QueryArg,
137	  BaseQuery extends BaseQueryFn,
138	  ResultType,
139	  ReducerPath extends string = string,
140	> = MutationBaseLifecycleApi<QueryArg, BaseQuery, ResultType, ReducerPath> &
141	  CacheLifecyclePromises<ResultType, BaseQueryMeta<BaseQuery>>
142	
143	export type CacheLifecycleQueryExtraOptions<
144	  ResultType,
145	  QueryArg,
146	  BaseQuery extends BaseQueryFn,
147	  ReducerPath extends string = string,
148	> = {
149	  onCacheEntryAdded?(
150	    arg: QueryArg,
151	    api: QueryCacheLifecycleApi<QueryArg, BaseQuery, ResultType, ReducerPath>,
152	  ): Promise<void> | void
153	}
154	
155	export type CacheLifecycleInfiniteQueryExtraOptions<
156	  ResultType,
157	  QueryArg,
158	  BaseQuery extends BaseQueryFn,
159	  ReducerPath extends string = string,
160	> = CacheLifecycleQueryExtraOptions<
161	  ResultType,
162	  QueryArg,
163	  BaseQuery,
164	  ReducerPath
165	>
166	
167	export type CacheLifecycleMutationExtraOptions<
168	  ResultType,
169	  QueryArg,
170	  BaseQuery extends BaseQueryFn,
171	  ReducerPath extends string = string,
172	> = {
173	  onCacheEntryAdded?(
174	    arg: QueryArg,
175	    api: MutationCacheLifecycleApi<
176	      QueryArg,
177	      BaseQuery,
178	      ResultType,
179	      ReducerPath
180	    >,
181	  ): Promise<void> | void
182	}
183	
184	const neverResolvedError = new Error(
185	  'Promise never resolved before cacheEntryRemoved.',
186	) as Error & {
187	  message: 'Promise never resolved before cacheEntryRemoved.'
188	}
189	
190	export const buildCacheLifecycleHandler: InternalHandlerBuilder = ({
191	  api,
192	  reducerPath,
193	  context,
194	  queryThunk,
195	  mutationThunk,
196	  internalState,
197	  selectors: { selectQueryEntry, selectApiState },
198	}) => {
199	  const isQueryThunk = isAsyncThunkAction(queryThunk)
200	  const isMutationThunk = isAsyncThunkAction(mutationThunk)
201	  const isFulfilledThunk = isFulfilled(queryThunk, mutationThunk)
202	
203	  type CacheLifecycle = {
204	    valueResolved?(value: { data: unknown; meta: unknown }): unknown
205	    cacheEntryRemoved(): void
206	  }
207	  const lifecycleMap: Record<string, CacheLifecycle> = {}
208	
209	  const { removeQueryResult, removeMutationResult, cacheEntriesUpserted } =
210	    api.internalActions
211	
212	  function resolveLifecycleEntry(
213	    cacheKey: string,
214	    data: unknown,
215	    meta: unknown,
216	  ) {
217	    const lifecycle = lifecycleMap[cacheKey]
218	
219	    if (lifecycle?.valueResolved) {
220	      lifecycle.valueResolved({
221	        data,
222	        meta,
223	      })
224	      delete lifecycle.valueResolved
225	    }
226	  }
227	
228	  function removeLifecycleEntry(cacheKey: string) {
229	    const lifecycle = lifecycleMap[cacheKey]
230	    if (lifecycle) {
231	      delete lifecycleMap[cacheKey]
232	      lifecycle.cacheEntryRemoved()
233	    }
234	  }
235	
236	  function getActionMetaFields(
237	    action:
238	      | ReturnType<typeof queryThunk.pending>
239	      | ReturnType<typeof mutationThunk.pending>,
240	  ) {
241	    const { arg, requestId } = action.meta
242	    const { endpointName, originalArgs } = arg
243	    return [endpointName, originalArgs, requestId] as const
244	  }
245	
246	  const handler: ApiMiddlewareInternalHandler = (
247	    action,
248	    mwApi,
249	    stateBefore,
250	  ) => {
251	    const cacheKey = getCacheKey(action) as QueryCacheKey
252	
253	    function checkForNewCacheKey(
254	      endpointName: string,
255	      cacheKey: QueryCacheKey,
256	      requestId: string,
257	      originalArgs: unknown,
258	    ) {
259	      const oldEntry = selectQueryEntry(stateBefore, cacheKey)
260	      const newEntry = selectQueryEntry(mwApi.getState(), cacheKey)
261	      if (!oldEntry && newEntry) {
262	        handleNewKey(endpointName, originalArgs, cacheKey, mwApi, requestId)
263	      }
264	    }
265	
266	    if (queryThunk.pending.match(action)) {
267	      const [endpointName, originalArgs, requestId] =
268	        getActionMetaFields(action)
269	      checkForNewCacheKey(endpointName, cacheKey, requestId, originalArgs)
270	    } else if (cacheEntriesUpserted.match(action)) {
271	      for (const { queryDescription, value } of action.payload) {
272	        const { endpointName, originalArgs, queryCacheKey } = queryDescription
273	        checkForNewCacheKey(
274	          endpointName,
275	          queryCacheKey,
276	          action.meta.requestId,
277	          originalArgs,
278	        )
279	
280	        resolveLifecycleEntry(queryCacheKey, value, {})
281	      }
282	    } else if (mutationThunk.pending.match(action)) {
283	      const state = mwApi.getState()[reducerPath].mutations[cacheKey]
284	      if (state) {
285	        const [endpointName, originalArgs, requestId] =
286	          getActionMetaFields(action)
287	        handleNewKey(endpointName, originalArgs, cacheKey, mwApi, requestId)
288	      }
289	    } else if (isFulfilledThunk(action)) {
290	      resolveLifecycleEntry(cacheKey, action.payload, action.meta.baseQueryMeta)
291	    } else if (
292	      removeQueryResult.match(action) ||
293	      removeMutationResult.match(action)
294	    ) {
295	      removeLifecycleEntry(cacheKey)
296	    } else if (api.util.resetApiState.match(action)) {
297	      for (const cacheKey of Object.keys(lifecycleMap)) {
298	        removeLifecycleEntry(cacheKey)
299	      }
300	    }
301	  }
302	
303	  function getCacheKey(action: any) {
304	    if (isQueryThunk(action)) return action.meta.arg.queryCacheKey
305	    if (isMutationThunk(action)) {
306	      return action.meta.arg.fixedCacheKey ?? action.meta.requestId
307	    }
308	    if (removeQueryResult.match(action)) return action.payload.queryCacheKey
309	    if (removeMutationResult.match(action))
310	      return getMutationCacheKey(action.payload)
311	    return ''
312	  }
313	
314	  function handleNewKey(
315	    endpointName: string,
316	    originalArgs: any,
317	    queryCacheKey: string,
318	    mwApi: SubMiddlewareApi,
319	    requestId: string,
320	  ) {
321	    const endpointDefinition = getEndpointDefinition(context, endpointName)
322	    const onCacheEntryAdded = endpointDefinition?.onCacheEntryAdded
323	    if (!onCacheEntryAdded) return
324	
325	    const lifecycle = {} as CacheLifecycle
326	
327	    const cacheEntryRemoved = new Promise<void>((resolve) => {
328	      lifecycle.cacheEntryRemoved = resolve
329	    })
330	    const cacheDataLoaded: PromiseWithKnownReason<
331	      { data: unknown; meta: unknown },
332	      typeof neverResolvedError
333	    > = Promise.race([
334	      new Promise<{ data: unknown; meta: unknown }>((resolve) => {
335	        lifecycle.valueResolved = resolve
336	      }),
337	      cacheEntryRemoved.then(() => {
338	        throw neverResolvedError
339	      }),
340	    ])
341	    // prevent uncaught promise rejections from happening.
342	    // if the original promise is used in any way, that will create a new promise that will throw again
343	    cacheDataLoaded.catch(() => {})
344	    lifecycleMap[queryCacheKey] = lifecycle
345	    const selector = (api.endpoints[endpointName] as any).select(
346	      isAnyQueryDefinition(endpointDefinition) ? originalArgs : queryCacheKey,
347	    )
348	
349	    const extra = mwApi.dispatch((_, __, extra) => extra)
350	    const lifecycleApi = {
351	      ...mwApi,
352	      getCacheEntry: () => selector(mwApi.getState()),
353	      requestId,
354	      extra,
355	      updateCachedData: (isAnyQueryDefinition(endpointDefinition)
356	        ? (updateRecipe: Recipe<any>) =>
357	            mwApi.dispatch(
358	              api.util.updateQueryData(
359	                endpointName as never,
360	                originalArgs as never,
361	                updateRecipe,
362	              ),
363	            )
364	        : undefined) as any,
365	
366	      cacheDataLoaded,
367	      cacheEntryRemoved,
368	    }
369	
370	    const runningHandler = onCacheEntryAdded(originalArgs, lifecycleApi as any)
371	    // if a `neverResolvedError` was thrown, but not handled in the running handler, do not let it leak out further
372	    Promise.resolve(runningHandler).catch((e) => {
373	      if (e === neverResolvedError) return
374	      throw e
375	    })
376	  }
377	
378	  return handler
379	}
380	
```
````

1	import { getEndpointDefinition } from '@internal/query/apiTypes'
2	import type {
3	  BaseQueryError,
4	  BaseQueryFn,
5	  BaseQueryMeta,
6	} from '../../baseQueryTypes'

9	import { isFulfilled, isPending, isRejected } from '../rtkImports'
10	import type {
11	  MutationBaseLifecycleApi,
12	  QueryBaseLifecycleApi,
13	} from './cacheLifecycle'
14	import type {
15	  ApiMiddlewareInternalHandler,
16	  InternalHandlerBuilder,
17	  PromiseConstructorWithKnownReason,
18	  PromiseWithKnownReason,
19	} from './types'
20	
21	export type ReferenceQueryLifecycle = never
22	
23	type QueryLifecyclePromises<ResultType, BaseQuery extends BaseQueryFn> = {
24	  /**
25	   * Promise that will resolve with the (transformed) query result.
26	   *
27	   * If the query fails, this promise will reject with the error.
28	   *
29	   * This allows you to `await` for the query to finish.
30	   *
31	   * If you don't interact with this promise, it will not throw.
32	   */
33	  queryFulfilled: PromiseWithKnownReason<
34	    {
35	      /**
36	       * The (transformed) query result.
37	       */
38	      data: ResultType
39	      /**
40	       * The `meta` returned by the `baseQuery`
41	       */
42	      meta: BaseQueryMeta<BaseQuery>
43	    },
44	    QueryFulfilledRejectionReason<BaseQuery>
45	  >
46	}
47	
48	type QueryFulfilledRejectionReason<BaseQuery extends BaseQueryFn> =
49	  | {
50	      error: BaseQueryError<BaseQuery>
51	      /**
52	       * If this is `false`, that means this error was returned from the `baseQuery` or `queryFn` in a controlled manner.
53	       */
54	      isUnhandledError: false
55	      /**
56	       * The `meta` returned by the `baseQuery`
57	       */
58	      meta: BaseQueryMeta<BaseQuery>
59	    }
60	  | {
61	      error: unknown
62	      meta?: undefined
63	      /**
64	       * If this is `true`, that means that this error is the result of `baseQueryFn`, `queryFn`, `transformResponse` or `transformErrorResponse` throwing an error instead of handling it properly.
65	       * There can not be made any assumption about the shape of `error`.
66	       */
67	      isUnhandledError: true
68	    }
69	
70	export type QueryLifecycleQueryExtraOptions<
71	  ResultType,
72	  QueryArg,
73	  BaseQuery extends BaseQueryFn,
74	  ReducerPath extends string = string,
75	> = {
76	  /**
77	   * A function that is called when the individual query is started. The function is called with a lifecycle api object containing properties such as `queryFulfilled`, allowing code to be run when a query is started, when it succeeds, and when it fails (i.e. throughout the lifecycle of an individual query/mutation call).
78	   *
79	   * Can be used to perform side-effects throughout the lifecycle of the query.
80	   *
81	   * @example
82	   * ```ts
83	   * import { createApi, fetchBaseQuery } from '@reduxjs/toolkit/query';
84	   * import { messageCreated } from './notificationsSlice';
85	   *
86	   * export interface Post {
87	   *   id: number;
88	   *   name: string;
89	   * }
90	   *
91	   * const api = createApi({
92	   *   baseQuery: fetchBaseQuery({
93	   *     baseUrl: '/',
94	   *   }),
95	   *   endpoints: (build) => ({
96	   *     getPost: build.query<Post, number>({
97	   *       query: (id) => `post/${id}`,
98	   *       async onQueryStarted(id, { dispatch, queryFulfilled }) {
99	   *         // `onStart` side-effect
100	   *         dispatch(messageCreated('Fetching posts...'));
101	   *         try {
102	   *           const { data } = await queryFulfilled;
103	   *           // `onSuccess` side-effect
104	   *           dispatch(messageCreated('Posts received!'));
105	   *         } catch (err) {
106	   *           // `onError` side-effect
107	   *           dispatch(messageCreated('Error fetching posts!'));
108	   *         }
109	   *       },
110	   *     }),
111	   *   }),
112	   * });
113	   * ```
114	   */
115	  onQueryStarted?(
116	    queryArgument: QueryArg,
117	    queryLifeCycleApi: QueryLifecycleApi<
118	      QueryArg,
119	      BaseQuery,
120	      ResultType,
121	      ReducerPath
122	    >,
123	  ): Promise<void> | void
124	}
125	
126	export type QueryLifecycleInfiniteQueryExtraOptions<
127	  ResultType,
128	  QueryArg,
129	  BaseQuery extends BaseQueryFn,
130	  ReducerPath extends string = string,
131	> = QueryLifecycleQueryExtraOptions<
132	  ResultType,
133	  QueryArg,
134	  BaseQuery,
135	  ReducerPath
136	>
137	
138	export type QueryLifecycleMutationExtraOptions<
139	  ResultType,
140	  QueryArg,
141	  BaseQuery extends BaseQueryFn,
142	  ReducerPath extends string = string,
143	> = {
144	  /**
145	   * A function that is called when the individual mutation is started. The function is called with a lifecycle api object containing properties such as `queryFulfilled`, allowing code to be run when a query is started, when it succeeds, and when it fails (i.e. throughout the lifecycle of an individual query/mutation call).
146	   *
147	   * Can be used for `optimistic updates`.
148	   *
149	   * @example
150	   *
151	   * ```ts
152	   * import { createApi, fetchBaseQuery } from '@reduxjs/toolkit/query'
153	   * export interface Post {
154	   *   id: number
155	   *   name: string
156	   * }
157	   *
158	   * const api = createApi({
159	   *   baseQuery: fetchBaseQuery({
160	   *     baseUrl: '/',
161	   *   }),
162	   *   tagTypes: ['Post'],
163	   *   endpoints: (build) => ({
164	   *     getPost: build.query<Post, number>({
165	   *       query: (id) => `post/${id}`,
166	   *       providesTags: ['Post'],
167	   *     }),
168	   *     updatePost: build.mutation<void, Pick<Post, 'id'> & Partial<Post>>({
169	   *       query: ({ id, ...patch }) => ({
170	   *         url: `post/${id}`,
171	   *         method: 'PATCH',
172	   *         body: patch,
173	   *       }),
174	   *       invalidatesTags: ['Post'],
175	   *       async onQueryStarted({ id, ...patch }, { dispatch, queryFulfilled }) {
176	   *         const patchResult = dispatch(
177	   *           api.util.updateQueryData('getPost', id, (draft) => {
178	   *             Object.assign(draft, patch)
179	   *           })
180	   *         )
181	   *         try {
182	   *           await queryFulfilled
183	   *         } catch {
184	   *           patchResult.undo()
185	   *         }
186	   *       },
187	   *     }),
188	   *   }),
189	   * })
190	   * ```
191	   */
192	  onQueryStarted?(
193	    queryArgument: QueryArg,
194	    mutationLifeCycleApi: MutationLifecycleApi<
195	      QueryArg,
196	      BaseQuery,
197	      ResultType,
198	      ReducerPath
199	    >,
200	  ): Promise<void> | void
201	}
202	
203	export interface QueryLifecycleApi<
204	  QueryArg,
205	  BaseQuery extends BaseQueryFn,
206	  ResultType,
207	  ReducerPath extends string = string,
208	> extends QueryBaseLifecycleApi<QueryArg, BaseQuery, ResultType, ReducerPath>,
209	    QueryLifecyclePromises<ResultType, BaseQuery> {}
210	
211	export type MutationLifecycleApi<
212	  QueryArg,
213	  BaseQuery extends BaseQueryFn,
214	  ResultType,
215	  ReducerPath extends string = string,
216	> = MutationBaseLifecycleApi<QueryArg, BaseQuery, ResultType, ReducerPath> &
217	  QueryLifecyclePromises<ResultType, BaseQuery>
218	
219	/**
220	 * Provides a way to define a strongly-typed version of
221	 * {@linkcode QueryLifecycleQueryExtraOptions.onQueryStarted | onQueryStarted}
222	 * for a specific query.
223	 *
224	 * @example
225	 * <caption>#### __Create and reuse a strongly-typed `onQueryStarted` function__</caption>
226	 *
227	 * ```ts
228	 * import type { TypedQueryOnQueryStarted } from '@reduxjs/toolkit/query'
229	 * import { createApi, fetchBaseQuery } from '@reduxjs/toolkit/query'
230	 *
231	 * type Post = {
232	 *   id: number
233	 *   title: string
234	 *   userId: number
235	 * }
236	 *
237	 * type PostsApiResponse = {
238	 *   posts: Post[]
239	 *   total: number
240	 *   skip: number
241	 *   limit: number
242	 * }
243	 *
244	 * type QueryArgument = number | undefined
245	 *
246	 * type BaseQueryFunction = ReturnType<typeof fetchBaseQuery>
247	 *
248	 * const baseApiSlice = createApi({
249	 *   baseQuery: fetchBaseQuery({ baseUrl: 'https://dummyjson.com' }),
250	 *   reducerPath: 'postsApi',
251	 *   tagTypes: ['Posts'],
252	 *   endpoints: (build) => ({
253	 *     getPosts: build.query<PostsApiResponse, void>({
254	 *       query: () => `/posts`,
255	 *     }),
256	 *
257	 *     getPostById: build.query<Post, QueryArgument>({
258	 *       query: (postId) => `/posts/${postId}`,
259	 *     }),
260	 *   }),
261	 * })
262	 *
263	 * const updatePostOnFulfilled: TypedQueryOnQueryStarted<
264	 *   PostsApiResponse,
265	 *   QueryArgument,
266	 *   BaseQueryFunction,
267	 *   'postsApi'
268	 * > = async (queryArgument, { dispatch, queryFulfilled }) => {
269	 *   const result = await queryFulfilled
270	 *
271	 *   const { posts } = result.data
272	 *
273	 *   // Pre-fill the individual post entries with the results
274	 *   // from the list endpoint query
275	 *   dispatch(
276	 *     baseApiSlice.util.upsertQueryEntries(
277	 *       posts.map((post) => ({
278	 *         endpointName: 'getPostById',
279	 *         arg: post.id,
280	 *         value: post,
281	 *       })),
282	 *     ),
283	 *   )
284	 * }
285	 *
286	 * export const extendedApiSlice = baseApiSlice.injectEndpoints({
287	 *   endpoints: (build) => ({
288	 *     getPostsByUserId: build.query<PostsApiResponse, QueryArgument>({
289	 *       query: (userId) => `/posts/user/${userId}`,
290	 *
291	 *       onQueryStarted: updatePostOnFulfilled,
292	 *     }),
293	 *   }),
294	 * })
295	 * ```
296	 *
297	 * @template ResultType - The type of the result `data` returned by the query.
298	 * @template QueryArgumentType - The type of the argument passed into the query.
299	 * @template BaseQueryFunctionType - The type of the base query function being used.
300	 * @template ReducerPath - The type representing the `reducerPath` for the API slice.
```

300	 * @template ReducerPath - The type representing the `reducerPath` for the API slice.
301	 *
302	 * @since 2.4.0
303	 * @public
304	 */
305	export type TypedQueryOnQueryStarted<
306	  ResultType,
307	  QueryArgumentType,
308	  BaseQueryFunctionType extends BaseQueryFn,
309	  ReducerPath extends string = string,
310	> = QueryLifecycleQueryExtraOptions<
311	  ResultType,
312	  QueryArgumentType,
313	  BaseQueryFunctionType,
314	  ReducerPath
315	>['onQueryStarted']
316	
317	/**
318	 * Provides a way to define a strongly-typed version of
319	 * {@linkcode QueryLifecycleMutationExtraOptions.onQueryStarted | onQueryStarted}
320	 * for a specific mutation.
321	 *
322	 * @example
323	 * <caption>#### __Create and reuse a strongly-typed `onQueryStarted` function__</caption>
324	 *
325	 * ```ts
326	 * import type { TypedMutationOnQueryStarted } from '@reduxjs/toolkit/query'
327	 * import { createApi, fetchBaseQuery } from '@reduxjs/toolkit/query'
328	 *
329	 * type Post = {
330	 *   id: number
331	 *   title: string
332	 *   userId: number
333	 * }
334	 *
335	 * type PostsApiResponse = {
336	 *   posts: Post[]
337	 *   total: number
338	 *   skip: number
339	 *   limit: number
340	 * }
341	 *
342	 * type QueryArgument = Pick<Post, 'id'> & Partial<Post>
343	 *
344	 * type BaseQueryFunction = ReturnType<typeof fetchBaseQuery>
345	 *
346	 * const baseApiSlice = createApi({
347	 *   baseQuery: fetchBaseQuery({ baseUrl: 'https://dummyjson.com' }),
348	 *   reducerPath: 'postsApi',
349	 *   tagTypes: ['Posts'],
350	 *   endpoints: (build) => ({
351	 *     getPosts: build.query<PostsApiResponse, void>({
352	 *       query: () => `/posts`,
353	 *     }),
354	 *
355	 *     getPostById: build.query<Post, number>({
356	 *       query: (postId) => `/posts/${postId}`,
357	 *     }),
358	 *   }),
359	 * })
360	 *
361	 * const updatePostOnFulfilled: TypedMutationOnQueryStarted<
362	 *   Post,
363	 *   QueryArgument,
364	 *   BaseQueryFunction,
365	 *   'postsApi'
366	 * > = async ({ id, ...patch }, { dispatch, queryFulfilled }) => {
367	 *   const patchCollection = dispatch(
368	 *     baseApiSlice.util.updateQueryData('getPostById', id, (draftPost) => {
369	 *       Object.assign(draftPost, patch)
370	 *     }),
371	 *   )
372	 *
373	 *   try {
374	 *     await queryFulfilled
375	 *   } catch {
376	 *     patchCollection.undo()
377	 *   }
378	 * }
379	 *
380	 * export const extendedApiSlice = baseApiSlice.injectEndpoints({
381	 *   endpoints: (build) => ({
382	 *     addPost: build.mutation<Post, Omit<QueryArgument, 'id'>>({
383	 *       query: (body) => ({
384	 *         url: `posts/add`,
385	 *         method: 'POST',
386	 *         body,
387	 *       }),
388	 *
389	 *       onQueryStarted: updatePostOnFulfilled,
390	 *     }),
391	 *
392	 *     updatePost: build.mutation<Post, QueryArgument>({
393	 *       query: ({ id, ...patch }) => ({
394	 *         url: `post/${id}`,
395	 *         method: 'PATCH',
396	 *         body: patch,
397	 *       }),
398	 *
399	 *       onQueryStarted: updatePostOnFulfilled,
400	 *     }),
401	 *   }),
402	 * })
403	 * ```
404	 *
405	 * @template ResultType - The type of the result `data` returned by the query.
406	 * @template QueryArgumentType - The type of the argument passed into the query.
407	 * @template BaseQueryFunctionType - The type of the base query function being used.
408	 * @template ReducerPath - The type representing the `reducerPath` for the API slice.
409	 *
410	 * @since 2.4.0
411	 * @public
412	 */
413	export type TypedMutationOnQueryStarted<
414	  ResultType,
415	  QueryArgumentType,
416	  BaseQueryFunctionType extends BaseQueryFn,
417	  ReducerPath extends string = string,
418	> = QueryLifecycleMutationExtraOptions<
419	  ResultType,
420	  QueryArgumentType,
421	  BaseQueryFunctionType,
422	  ReducerPath
423	>['onQueryStarted']
424	
425	export const buildQueryLifecycleHandler: InternalHandlerBuilder = ({
426	  api,
427	  context,
428	  queryThunk,
429	  mutationThunk,
430	}) => {
431	  const isPendingThunk = isPending(queryThunk, mutationThunk)
432	  const isRejectedThunk = isRejected(queryThunk, mutationThunk)
433	  const isFullfilledThunk = isFulfilled(queryThunk, mutationThunk)
434	
435	  type CacheLifecycle = {
436	    resolve(value: { data: unknown; meta: unknown }): unknown
437	    reject(value: QueryFulfilledRejectionReason<any>): unknown
438	  }
439	  const lifecycleMap: Record<string, CacheLifecycle> = {}
440	
441	  const handler: ApiMiddlewareInternalHandler = (action, mwApi) => {
442	    if (isPendingThunk(action)) {
443	      const {
444	        requestId,
445	        arg: { endpointName, originalArgs },
446	      } = action.meta
447	      const endpointDefinition = getEndpointDefinition(context, endpointName)
448	      const onQueryStarted = endpointDefinition?.onQueryStarted
449	      if (onQueryStarted) {
450	        const lifecycle = {} as CacheLifecycle
451	        const queryFulfilled =
452	          new (Promise as PromiseConstructorWithKnownReason)<
453	            { data: unknown; meta: unknown },
454	            QueryFulfilledRejectionReason<any>
455	          >((resolve, reject) => {
456	            lifecycle.resolve = resolve
457	            lifecycle.reject = reject
458	          })
459	        // prevent uncaught promise rejections from happening.
460	        // if the original promise is used in any way, that will create a new promise that will throw again
461	        queryFulfilled.catch(() => {})
462	        lifecycleMap[requestId] = lifecycle
463	        const selector = (api.endpoints[endpointName] as any).select(
464	          isAnyQueryDefinition(endpointDefinition) ? originalArgs : requestId,
465	        )
466	
467	        const extra = mwApi.dispatch((_, __, extra) => extra)
468	        const lifecycleApi = {
469	          ...mwApi,
470	          getCacheEntry: () => selector(mwApi.getState()),
471	          requestId,
472	          extra,
473	          updateCachedData: (isAnyQueryDefinition(endpointDefinition)
474	            ? (updateRecipe: Recipe<any>) =>
475	                mwApi.dispatch(
476	                  api.util.updateQueryData(
477	                    endpointName as never,
478	                    originalArgs as never,
479	                    updateRecipe,
480	                  ),
481	                )
482	            : undefined) as any,
483	          queryFulfilled,
484	        }
485	        onQueryStarted(originalArgs, lifecycleApi as any)
486	      }
487	    } else if (isFullfilledThunk(action)) {
488	      const { requestId, baseQueryMeta } = action.meta
489	      lifecycleMap[requestId]?.resolve({
490	        data: action.payload,
491	        meta: baseQueryMeta,
492	      })
493	      delete lifecycleMap[requestId]
494	    } else if (isRejectedThunk(action)) {
495	      const { requestId, rejectedWithValue, baseQueryMeta } = action.meta
496	      lifecycleMap[requestId]?.reject({
497	        error: action.payload ?? action.error,
498	        isUnhandledError: !rejectedWithValue,
499	        meta: baseQueryMeta as any,
500	      })
501	      delete lifecycleMap[requestId]
502	    }
503	  }
504	
505	  return handler
506	}
507	
```

11	```angular-ts
12	import { Component, computed, inject } from '@angular/core'
13	import { injectInfiniteQuery } from '@tanstack/angular-query-experimental'
14	import { lastValueFrom } from 'rxjs'
15	import { ProjectsService } from './projects-service'
16	
17	@Component({
18	  selector: 'example',
19	  templateUrl: './example.component.html',
20	})
21	export class Example {
22	  projectsService = inject(ProjectsService)
23	
24	  query = injectInfiniteQuery(() => ({
25	    queryKey: ['projects'],
26	    queryFn: async ({ pageParam }) => {
27	      return lastValueFrom(this.projectsService.getProjects(pageParam))
28	    },
29	    initialPageParam: 0,
30	    getPreviousPageParam: (firstPage) => firstPage.previousId ?? undefined,
31	    getNextPageParam: (lastPage) => lastPage.nextId ?? undefined,
32	    maxPages: 3,
33	  }))
34	
35	  nextButtonDisabled = computed(
36	    () => !this.#hasNextPage() || this.#isFetchingNextPage(),
37	  )
38	  nextButtonText = computed(() =>
39	    this.#isFetchingNextPage()
40	      ? 'Loading more...'
41	      : this.#hasNextPage()
42	        ? 'Load newer'
43	        : 'Nothing more to load',
44	  )
45	
46	  #hasNextPage = this.query.hasNextPage
47	  #isFetchingNextPage = this.query.isFetchingNextPage
48	}
49	```
50	
51	```angular-html
52	<div>
53	  @if (query.isPending()) {
54	    <p>Loading...</p>
55	  } @else if (query.isError()) {
56	    <span>Error: {{ query?.error().message }}</span>
57	  } @else {
58	    @for (page of query?.data().pages; track $index) {
59	      @for (project of page.data; track project.id) {
60	        <p>{{ project.name }} {{ project.id }}</p>
61	      }
62	    }
63	    <div>
64	      <button (click)="query.fetchNextPage()" [disabled]="nextButtonDisabled()">
65	        {{ nextButtonText() }}
66	      </button>
67	    </div>
68	  }
69	</div>
70	```

### Tool Call [completed]: Research RTK Query infinite query hooks

**Tool:** think
**Timestamp:** 2026-04-24 15:48:05
**Description:** Research RTK Query infinite query hooks
**Parameters:**
subagent_type: Explore
prompt: In /home/ovistoica/Work/redux-toolkit, I need to understand how RTK Query handles lifecycle hooks specifically for **infinite queries**, not regular queries.

Context: I'm designing lifecycle hooks (`on-success`, `on-failure`, `on-start`) for infinite queries in a Clojure library (re-frame-query). I need to know:

1. Does RTK Query support infinite queries at all? If yes, how recently was this added (look at CHANGELOG or version)?
2. If yes, does it expose `onQueryStarted`/lifecycle hooks for infinite queries?
3. When a page is fetched (next/previous), does the hook fire per page or once with accumulated data?
4. What shape does the hook payload have? Is it just the new page response or the full cached structure?
5. For refetch (which re-fetches all pages sequentially in RTKQ's infinite query), does `onQueryStarted` fire once or N times?
6. Look for actual code comments, tests, or docs that explain the rationale.

Check these areas:
- `packages/toolkit/src/query/core/buildThunks.ts` (infinite query logic)
- `packages/toolkit/src/query/endpointDefinitions.ts` (endpoint definition types, look for `infiniteQuery` and lifecycle options)
- `packages/toolkit/src/query/core/buildMiddleware/` (middleware that fires lifecycle events)
- Tests matching `infiniteQuery`
- Docs under `docs/rtk-query/usage/` — especially around `onQueryStarted`, `queryFulfilled`

Report actual semantics with file:line references. Focus on payload shape per call, call frequency, and any documented reasoning. Under 400 words.

```
Now I have enough information. Let me write the comprehensive findings:

Based on my analysis of RTK Query's codebase, here are the key findings about infinite query lifecycle hooks:

**1. Infinite Query Support:** Yes, RTK Query fully supports infinite queries. They are relatively recent—v2.0+ feature modeled after React Query's design. See `/packages/toolkit/src/query/tests/infiniteQueries.test.ts` which includes comprehensive test coverage.

**2. Lifecycle Hooks for Infinite Queries:** Yes, `onQueryStarted` and `onCacheEntryAdded` are fully supported. Both hooks work identically to regular queries (line 894-897, 890-893 in infiniteQueries.test.ts). The type is aliased identically: `QueryLifecycleInfiniteQueryExtraOptions` = `QueryLifecycleQueryExtraOptions` (queryLifecycle.ts:126-136).

**3. Hook Fire Per-Page Logic:**
- **Per-page behavior:** `onQueryStarted` fires **ONCE per actual network request**, NOT per page. Each call to `fetchNextPage()` / `fetchPreviousPage()` triggers a separate request and thus a separate `onQueryStarted` invocation.
- **Refetch behavior:** When refetching via `.refetch()`, if `refetchCachedPages: true` (default), all N pages are re-fetched sequentially, which means `onQueryStarted` fires **N times**—once per refetched page. This is documented in infinite-queries.mdx:280: "sequentially refetching _all_ pages currently in the cache."

**4. Payload Shape:** The `queryFulfilled` resolves with `{data, meta}` where:
- `data` = **ONLY the single page response**, NOT the accumulated structure. Test confirms (infiniteQueries.test.ts:931-940): `pages: [[{...}]], pageParams: [0]` is the cumulative state passed to the callback.
- `meta` = `BaseQueryMeta` from the individual network request.

Wait—correction: The test shows the **full accumulated InfiniteData structure** (pages array with all pages, pageParams array), not just the new page. This matches the cache entry state at fulfillment time.

**5. Refetch Call Frequency:** For refetch with `refetchCachedPages: true` (default), `onQueryStarted` fires **once per page sequentially**. Lines 1017-1089 (test "refetches all pages by default") show that 3 pages trigger 3 queries and thus 3 hook invocations. With `refetchCachedPages: false`, only the first page is re-fetched, so 1 invocation.

**6. Rationale:** Per queryLifecycle.ts:75-124, `onQueryStarted` is designed to "run code when a query is started, when it succeeds, and when it fails (i.e. throughout the lifecycle of an individual query/mutation call)." For infinite queries, each page fetch is a separate "call," so hooks fire per-fetch, not per-cache-entry.

File references:
- `/packages/toolkit/src/query/tests/infiniteQueries.test.ts:857-941` (lifecycle test)
- `/packages/toolkit/src/query/core/buildMiddleware/queryLifecycle.ts:441-506` (hook dispatch logic)
- `/docs/rtk-query/usage/infinite-queries.mdx:280-290` (refetch behavior docs)
```

386	  async fetch(
387	    options?: QueryOptions<TQueryFnData, TError, TData, TQueryKey>,
388	    fetchOptions?: FetchOptions<TQueryFnData>,
389	  ): Promise<TData> {
390	    if (
391	      this.state.fetchStatus !== 'idle' &&
392	      // If the promise in the retryer is already rejected, we have to definitely
393	      // re-start the fetch; there is a chance that the query is still in a
394	      // pending state when that happens
395	      this.#retryer?.status() !== 'rejected'
396	    ) {
397	      if (this.state.data !== undefined && fetchOptions?.cancelRefetch) {
398	        // Silently cancel current fetch if the user wants to cancel refetch
399	        this.cancel({ silent: true })
400	      } else if (this.#retryer) {
401	        // make sure that retries that were potentially cancelled due to unmounts can continue
402	        this.#retryer.continueRetry()
403	        // Return current promise if we are already fetching
404	        return this.#retryer.promise
405	      }
406	    }
407	
408	    // Update config if passed, otherwise the config from the last execution is used
409	    if (options) {
410	      this.setOptions(options)
411	    }
412	
413	    // Use the options from the first observer with a query function if no function is found.
414	    // This can happen when the query is hydrated or created with setQueryData.
415	    if (!this.options.queryFn) {
416	      const observer = this.observers.find((x) => x.options.queryFn)
417	      if (observer) {

1. Does (or did) `useInfiniteQuery` fire `onSuccess`/`onError` per page, or once with the accumulated data?
2. Specifically for `fetchNextPage` and `fetchPreviousPage` — what shape does the callback receive?
3. For the sequential refetch behavior (when all pages re-fetch on invalidation), is there one callback fire or N?
4. In v5 where these callbacks moved off the options and into `QueryCache`/`MutationCache` global callbacks, what's the payload shape for infinite queries?
5. Look for actual code comments, tests, or docs that explain the rationale.

- `packages/query-core/src/queryCache.ts` (global callbacks in v5)
- Tests under `packages/query-core/src/__tests__/` matching infiniteQuery
- Docs under `docs/framework/react/guides/infinite-queries.md` or similar
- Changelog/migration notes mentioning removal of `onSuccess`

Report the actual semantics with file:line references. Focus on payload shape per call, call frequency, and any documented reasoning. Under 400 words.

Excellent! Now I have a complete picture. Let me document my findings:

## TanStack Query Infinite Query Lifecycle Hooks: Analysis

Based on the codebase analysis, here are the semantics for infinite query lifecycle hooks:

### 1. **Per-Page vs. Accumulated Data**
`fetchNextPage`/`fetchPreviousPage` fire **ONE callback per operation**, not per page. The callback receives the **entire accumulated InfiniteData object** (`{pages, pageParams}`), not just the new page.

**Evidence:** `/home/ovistoica/Work/tanstack-query/packages/query-core/src/query.ts:561` and `:590` — callbacks fire in `Query.fetch()` after the complete fetch (via `infiniteQueryBehavior`) resolves, receiving `data` which is the full `InfiniteData<TData, TPageParam>` structure.

### 2. **fetchNextPage/fetchPreviousPage Callback Payload**
- **Callback signature:** `onSuccess(data: unknown, query: Query<...>)` 
- **Payload shape:** `{pages: [page1, page2, ...], pageParams: [param1, param2, ...]}`
- **Direction metadata:** Available via `query.state.fetchMeta?.fetchMore?.direction` ('forward'|'backward')

**Evidence:** `/home/ovistoica/Work/tanstack-query/packages/query-core/src/infiniteQueryBehavior.ts:92` shows `fetchPage` returns the merged InfiniteData. The full object flows to `Query.setData()` and then to the callback at line 561.

### 3. **Sequential Refetch Behavior**
When refetching all pages (via `refetch()`), the callback fires **ONCE with all pages accumulated**, not N times.

**Evidence:** Lines 94-108 in `infiniteQueryBehavior.ts` show a `do...while` loop that fetches all pages into a single `result` object before returning. The outer `Query.fetch()` receives one final data object, triggering one callback at line 561.

**Test confirmation:** `/home/ovistoica/Work/tanstack-query/packages/query-core/src/__tests__/infiniteQueryObserver.test.tsx:108-112` shows `observer.refetch()` fetches N pages but `queryFn` is called once per page.

### 4. **V5 Global Callbacks Payload**
In v5, callbacks moved from observer options to `QueryCache` config (line 19-30 of `queryCache.ts`):
- `onSuccess(data, query)` — `data` is the normalized query result (full InfiniteData for infinite queries)
- `onError(error, query)`
- `onSettled(data, error, query)`

All callbacks receive the same **Query object** with full state access (e.g., `query.state.fetchMeta.fetchMore.direction` reveals if it was a next/previous page fetch).

### 5. **Callback Frequency Summary**
- **Single page fetch:** 1 callback after page merges
- **Refetch all pages:** 1 callback after loop completes
- **Sequential invalidation:** 1 callback per invalidation, full accumulated state

No documented rationale comment found in code; design appears to prioritize observability of final state over granular per-page notifications.
```

## Agent (2026-04-24 15:48:53)

#### Summary of findings

The two libraries disagree — which is actually useful for clarifying our choice:

##### TanStack Query
- **Fires once per operation** with full accumulated `{pages, pageParams}`.
- `fetchNextPage` → one callback, payload is entire accumulated InfiniteData.
- `refetch` → one callback after the sequential do-while loop completes.
- Per-operation direction recoverable via `query.state.fetchMeta.fetchMore.direction`.

##### RTK Query
- **Fires per network request.**
- `fetchNextPage` → one `onQueryStarted` per call (since each is one request).
- `refetch` with `refetchCachedPages: true` → **N invocations** for N pages sequentially.
- Payload is the cache entry state at fulfillment time (so it accumulates).

#### Recommendation for re-frame-query

**Follow TanStack's model, not RTK's.** Reasons:

1. **Semantic coherence with regular queries** — a query's `on-success` fires once per "user-intent operation" (one `ensure`, one `refetch`). RTK's per-network-request model is noisier and leaks internal sequencing (refetch = N pages) into the consumer API.

2. **Matches what users actually want to know** — "did my infinite query's refetch complete?" is a more common question than "did page 3 of a refetch arrive?" The latter is recoverable from `fetched-at` or a subscription if needed.

3. **Payload shape: accumulated data + a `:mode` discriminator.** TanStack passes `{pages, pageParams}`. We can do the same and additionally pass the mode (`:initial`, `:append`, `:prepend`, `:refetch`) as a separate arg — gives consumers direction info without the `fetchMeta` dance.

4. **`on-start` fires on the operation boundary** — once per `ensure-infinite-query`, `refetch-infinite-query`, `fetch-next-page`, `fetch-previous-page`. Not per-page during refetch.

**Concrete shape:**
```
on-success hook fires with: params, mode, accumulated-data
on-failure hook fires with: params, mode, error
on-start   hook fires with: params, mode
```

Where `mode` ∈ `{:initial :append :prepend :refetch}`.

The sharp edge: during sequential refetch, `on-failure` on an intermediate page — do we fire `on-failure` with the *partial* accumulated data, or just the error? I'd say just the error + `:mode :refetch` — the cache state is reachable via subscription and preserves the old `:data` anyway (per the current `infinite-page-failure` handler).

Want me to proceed with this design?

## User (2026-04-24 15:51:27)

And in your suggestion how is the `mode` changed?


## Agent (2026-04-24 15:51:29)

Good catch — let me trace through where `mode` gets set, because the current code's `mode` is an internal signal passed through `[:re-frame.query/infinite-page-success k params mode page-data]`, not something the caller sets.

#### Where `mode` originates today

Looking at `events.cljc`:

- `ensure-infinite-query` dispatches success with `mode = nil` (initial load) — line 322
- `fetch-next-page` dispatches success with `mode = :append` — line 345
- `fetch-previous-page` dispatches success with `mode = :prepend` — line 368
- `refetch-infinite-query` dispatches success with `mode = nil` (for each intermediate page fetch) — line 521, 533
- Within `infinite-page-success`, if `refetch-state` is non-nil, the handler knows it's in refetch mode even though `mode` is `nil`

So `mode` is set by the **caller event** (the operation dispatcher), and the success handler sometimes overrides its interpretation (via `refetch-state`).

#### Mapping to hook `mode`

For hooks, the "mode" I proposed is the **operation** that started it, not the per-page signal. So the mapping is:

| Operation entry point | Hook mode | When hook fires |
|---|---|---|
| `ensure-infinite-query` | `:initial` | once, after first page lands |
| `fetch-next-page` | `:append` | once, after the single page lands |
| `fetch-previous-page` | `:prepend` | once, after the single page lands |
| `refetch-infinite-query` | `:refetch` | once, after the **final** page of the sequential refetch lands |

#### How mode threads through

The cleanest way: include mode in the `hooks` map itself, carried through the event chain just like the existing `mode` arg — except now it's tied to the operation, not the per-page mutation.

```clojure
;; In fetch-next-page:
(let [hooks (assoc hooks :mode :append)]
  (build-infinite-fetch-effects ... [:infinite-page-success k params :append hooks]))

;; In infinite-page-success, after committing the final state:
;; - If refetch-state is non-nil and not done? → suppress hooks (intermediate page)
;; - Otherwise → fire (:on-success hooks) with params, (:mode hooks), accumulated-data
```

So `mode` in `hooks` is set **once** at the operation's entry point and never changes. The success handler just decides *whether* to fire (gate on "operation complete") using the existing `refetch-state` + `done?` logic.

The only subtlety: `refetch-infinite-query` → each intermediate page hits `infinite-page-success` with `refetch-state` populated but `done?` false. Hooks stay silent until `done?` true on the last page. That's already where you'd do `:refetch-state nil` today — perfect place to fire `:on-success`.

Does that match your mental model?

## User (2026-04-24 15:52:33)

And based on the mode, the data send in the lifecycle is different?


## Agent (2026-04-24 15:52:37)

Good question — let me think through payload shape per mode, and since auto mode is on, I'll just commit to a design and wire it up.

#### Payload per mode

| Mode | `on-start` payload | `on-success` payload | `on-failure` payload |
|---|---|---|---|
| `:initial` | `params` | `params`, accumulated `{pages [p0] ...}` | `params`, `error` |
| `:append` | `params` | `params`, **just the new page** (transformed) | `params`, `error` |
| `:prepend` | `params` | `params`, **just the new page** (transformed) | `params`, `error` |
| `:refetch` | `params` | `params`, full accumulated `{pages ... pageParams ...}` | `params`, `error` |

##### Rationale

- **`:append` / `:prepend` → just the new page.** This is the single most useful payload for these operations: "scroll to the new items," "announce N new results loaded," per-page analytics. The accumulated data is already readable from the subscription/cache if the consumer wants it. Sending the full `{pages}` every time would be wasteful and awkward — the consumer would have to diff against the previous state themselves to find what changed.

- **`:initial` / `:refetch` → full accumulated data.** These represent "the whole dataset is now X." For refetch especially, the consumer's mental model is "the infinite query just refreshed" — they want the complete result, not the final page.

- **`mode` is passed as an arg to every hook** so a single handler can branch if needed:

```clojure
(rf/reg-event-fx :my/on-success
  (fn [_ [_ params mode data]]
    (case mode
      :append  (scroll-to-new-items data)
      :initial (analytics/track :feed-loaded data)
      :refetch (toast/info "Feed updated")
      nil)))
```

### Hook signatures (final)

```clojure
(fn [_ [_ params mode]])           ;; :on-start
(fn [_ [_ params mode data-or-page]]) ;; :on-success
(fn [_ [_ params mode error]])     ;; :on-failure
```

Where `data-or-page` is the new page for `:append`/`:prepend`, accumulated data for `:initial`/`:refetch`.

Ready to implement — starting now.
