file_path: /home/ovistoica/workspace/re-frame-query/test/re_frame/query/events_test.cljc
old_string: (deftest query-hooks-receive-transformed-data-test
  (testing "on-success hook receives data after :transform-response is applied"
    (rf-test/run-test-sync
     (let [hook-data (atom nil)]
       (rf/reg-event-db :test/on-success
         (fn [db [_ _params data]] (reset! hook-data data) db))
       (rfq/set-default-effect-fn! h/noop-effect-fn)
       (rfq/reg-query :books/list
         {:query-fn (fn [_] {})
          :transform-response (fn [response _params] (:data response))})
       (rf/dispatch [:re-frame.query/query-success :books/list {}
                     {:on-success [[:test/on-success]]}
                     {:data [{:id 1}] :meta {:total 1}}])
       (is (= [{:id 1}] @hook-data)
           "hook receives the unwrapped :data, not the raw response"))))

  (testing "on-failure hook receives error after :transform-error is applied"
    (rf-test/run-test-sync
     (let [hook-error (atom nil)]
       (rf/reg-event-db :test/on-failure
         (fn [db [_ _params error]] (reset! hook-error error) db))
       (rfq/set-default-effect-fn! h/noop-effect-fn)
       (rfq/reg-query :books/list
         {:query-fn (fn [_] {})
          :transform-error (fn [error _params]
                             {:code (:status error) :msg (:body error)})})
       (rf/dispatch [:re-frame.query/query-failure :books/list {}
                     {:on-failure [[:test/on-failure]]}
                     {:status 500 :body "boom"}])
       (is (= {:code 500 :msg "boom"} @hook-error)
           "hook receives the normalized error shape")))))
new_string: (deftest query-hooks-receive-transformed-data-test
  (testing "on-success hook receives data after :transform-response is applied"
    (rf-test/run-test-sync
     (let [hook-data (atom nil)]
       (rf/reg-event-db :test/on-success
         (fn [db [_ _params data]] (reset! hook-data data) db))
       (rf/reg-fx :test/fake-http-success
         (fn [{:keys [on-success response]}]
           (rf/dispatch (conj on-success response))))
       (rfq/set-default-effect-fn!
        (fn [request on-success _on-failure]
          {:test/fake-http-success (assoc request :on-success on-success)}))
       (rfq/reg-query :books/list
         {:query-fn (fn [_] {:response {:data [{:id 1}] :meta {:total 1}}})
          :transform-response (fn [response _params] (:data response))})
       (rf/dispatch [:re-frame.query/ensure-query :books/list {}
                     {:on-success [[:test/on-success]]}])
       (is (= [{:id 1}] @hook-data)
           "hook receives the unwrapped :data, not the raw response"))))

  (testing "on-failure hook receives error after :transform-error is applied"
    (rf-test/run-test-sync
     (let [hook-error (atom nil)]
       (rf/reg-event-db :test/on-failure
         (fn [db [_ _params error]] (reset! hook-error error) db))
       (rf/reg-fx :test/fake-http-failure
         (fn [{:keys [on-failure error]}]
           (rf/dispatch (conj on-failure error))))
       (rfq/set-default-effect-fn!
        (fn [request _on-success on-failure]
          {:test/fake-http-failure (assoc request :on-failure on-failure)}))
       (rfq/reg-query :books/list
         {:query-fn (fn [_] {:error {:status 500 :body "boom"}})
          :transform-error (fn [error _params]
                             {:code (:status error) :msg (:body error)})})
       (rf/dispatch [:re-frame.query/ensure-query :books/list {}
                     {:on-failure [[:test/on-failure]]}])
       (is (= {:code 500 :msg "boom"} @hook-error)
           "hook receives the normalized error shape")))))

Let me also upgrade `query-hooks-fire-only-on-matching-outcome-test` to exercise the real path — currently it dispatches `query-success` / `query-failure` directly:

### Tool Call [completed]: Edit test/re_frame/query/events_test.cljc

**Tool:** edit
**Timestamp:** 2026-04-24 16:24:17
**Parameters:**
file_path: /home/ovistoica/workspace/re-frame-query/test/re_frame/query/events_test.cljc
old_string: (deftest query-hooks-fire-only-on-matching-outcome-test
  (testing "on-success does not fire on failure, on-failure does not fire on success"
    (rf-test/run-test-sync
     (let [success-calls (atom 0)
           failure-calls (atom 0)]
       (rf/reg-event-db :test/on-success
         (fn [db _] (swap! success-calls inc) db))
       (rf/reg-event-db :test/on-failure
         (fn [db _] (swap! failure-calls inc) db))
       (rfq/set-default-effect-fn! h/noop-effect-fn)
       (rfq/reg-query :books/list {:query-fn (fn [_] {})})
       ;; Success path
       (rf/dispatch [:re-frame.query/query-success :books/list {}
                     {:on-success [[:test/on-success]]
                      :on-failure [[:test/on-failure]]}
                     [{:id 1}]])
       (is (= 1 @success-calls) "on-success fired once")
       (is (= 0 @failure-calls) "on-failure did NOT fire on success")
       ;; Failure path
       (rf/dispatch [:re-frame.query/query-failure :books/list {}
                     {:on-success [[:test/on-success]]
                      :on-failure [[:test/on-failure]]}
                     {:status 500}])
       (is (= 1 @success-calls) "on-success still at 1 after failure")
       (is (= 1 @failure-calls) "on-failure fired once")))))
new_string: (deftest query-hooks-fire-only-on-matching-outcome-test
  (testing "on-success fires on success path only; on-failure fires on failure path only"
    (rf-test/run-test-sync
     (let [success-calls (atom 0)
           failure-calls (atom 0)
           outcome (atom :success)]
       (rf/reg-event-db :test/on-success
         (fn [db _] (swap! success-calls inc) db))
       (rf/reg-event-db :test/on-failure
         (fn [db _] (swap! failure-calls inc) db))
       ;; Test effect branches on the current @outcome — either fires on-success or on-failure
       (rf/reg-fx :test/fake-http
         (fn [{:keys [on-success on-failure response error]}]
           (if (= @outcome :success)
             (rf/dispatch (conj on-success response))
             (rf/dispatch (conj on-failure error)))))
       (rfq/set-default-effect-fn!
        (fn [request on-success on-failure]
          {:test/fake-http (assoc request :on-success on-success :on-failure on-failure)}))
       (rfq/reg-query :books/list
         {:query-fn (fn [_] {:response [{:id 1}] :error {:status 500}})})
       ;; Success path
       (reset! outcome :success)
       (rf/dispatch [:re-frame.query/ensure-query :books/list {}
                     {:on-success [[:test/on-success]]
                      :on-failure [[:test/on-failure]]}])
       (is (= 1 @success-calls) "on-success fired once on success path")
       (is (= 0 @failure-calls) "on-failure did NOT fire on success path")
       ;; Failure path — invalidate cache first so ensure-query refetches
       (rf/dispatch [:re-frame.query/invalidate-tags #{[:forces-refetch]}])
       (reset! outcome :failure)
       (rf/dispatch [:re-frame.query/refetch-query :books/list {}
                     {:on-success [[:test/on-success]]
                      :on-failure [[:test/on-failure]]}])
       (is (= 1 @success-calls) "on-success stayed at 1 after failure path")
       (is (= 1 @failure-calls) "on-failure fired once on failure path")))))

And the optimistic-update-cycle test — also upgrade to real flow:

### Tool Call [completed]: Edit test/re_frame/query/events_test.cljc

**Tool:** edit

**Parameters:**
file_path: /home/ovistoica/workspace/re-frame-query/test/re_frame/query/events_test.cljc
old_string: (deftest query-hooks-optimistic-update-cycle-test
  (testing "Full optimistic cycle via query hooks: on-success patches another query"
    (rf-test/run-test-sync
     (let [side-effects (atom [])]
        ;; This hook observes the :books/list success and triggers a cache patch
        ;; for :books/count based on the loaded data.
       (rf/reg-event-fx :test/sync-count
         (fn [_ [_ _params data]]
           (swap! side-effects conj {:saw-data data})
           {:dispatch [:re-frame.query/set-query-data
                       :books/count {} {:count (count data)}]}))
       (rfq/set-default-effect-fn! h/noop-effect-fn)
       (rfq/reg-query :books/list {:query-fn (fn [_] {})})
       (rfq/reg-query :books/count {:query-fn (fn [_] {})})
       (rf/dispatch [:re-frame.query/query-success :books/list {}
                     {:on-success [[:test/sync-count]]}
                     [{:id 1} {:id 2} {:id 3}]])
       (is (= [{:saw-data [{:id 1} {:id 2} {:id 3}]}] @side-effects)
           "hook saw the query's data")
       (is (= {:count 3}
              @(rf/subscribe [:re-frame.query/query-data :books/count {}]))
           "hook-dispatched event patched the other query's cache")))))
new_string: (deftest query-hooks-optimistic-update-cycle-test
  (testing "Full cycle: ensure-query → fake effect → on-success hook patches another query"
    (rf-test/run-test-sync
     (let [side-effects (atom [])]
       ;; Hook observes :books/list success and patches :books/count cache
       (rf/reg-event-fx :test/sync-count
         (fn [_ [_ _params data]]
           (swap! side-effects conj {:saw-data data})
           {:dispatch [:re-frame.query/set-query-data
                       :books/count {} {:count (count data)}]}))
       (rf/reg-fx :test/fake-http-success
         (fn [{:keys [on-success response]}]
           (rf/dispatch (conj on-success response))))
       (rfq/set-default-effect-fn!
        (fn [request on-success _on-failure]
          {:test/fake-http-success (assoc request :on-success on-success)}))
       (rfq/reg-query :books/list
         {:query-fn (fn [_] {:response [{:id 1} {:id 2} {:id 3}]})})
       (rfq/reg-query :books/count {:query-fn (fn [_] {})})
       (rf/dispatch [:re-frame.query/ensure-query :books/list {}
                     {:on-success [[:test/sync-count]]}])
       (is (= [{:saw-data [{:id 1} {:id 2} {:id 3}]}] @side-effects)
           "hook saw the query's data after the fake effect fired")
       (is (= {:count 3}
              @(rf/subscribe [:re-frame.query/query-data :books/count {}]))
           "hook-dispatched event patched the other query's cache")))))

## Agent (2026-04-24 16:25:06)

All 112 tests still pass with the stronger end-to-end flow.

The key improvement: each test now registers a `:test/fake-http-success` or `:test/fake-http-failure` effect that **synchronously dispatches** the `on-success` / `on-failure` event vector (with its appended response/error). This proves the actual chain:

1. Caller dispatches `ensure-query` (or `refetch-query`) with `{:on-success [...]}` opts
2. Event handler calls `build-query-effects` which passes hooks to `effect-fn`
3. `effect-fn` produces an effect map containing `:on-success [:re-frame.query/query-success k params hooks]`
4. The fake effect fires, conjs the response, dispatches the event
5. `query-success` handler receives hooks and runs `dispatch-hooks` → user hook fires

If any link in that chain is broken, the tests fail. The previous version only tested step 5 in isolation.
## Agent (2026-04-24 16:25:15)

Tests upgraded and passing. Ready for the next step — presumably wiring up infinite query hooks based on the `:initial` / `:append` / `:prepend` / `:refetch` mode design we settled on.

## Agent (2026-04-24 16:31:55)

Two key decisions for `on-start`:

1. **Where to fire it?** Both `ensure-query` (when it actually fetches — i.e., inside the `if stale? and not fetching?` branch) and `refetch-query`. Not `poll-refetch`. **Critical:** it should NOT fire if the cache hit (stale? is false) — because no fetch happens, there's no "start" to observe. This mirrors mutation on-start which fires before the effect, and for queries there's no effect when cache hits.

2. **Shape of hooks map.** The stored `hooks` in `select-keys` would ideally include `:on-start` too, but `on-start` doesn't need to thread through to the success/failure event — it only fires once at dispatch time. So it stays in `opts`, not in the hooks map threaded through the effect.

Let me update:

### Tool Call [completed]: Edit src/re_frame/query/events.cljc

**Tool:** edit
**Timestamp:** 2026-04-24 16:32:07
**Parameters:**
file_path: /home/ovistoica/workspace/re-frame-query/src/re_frame/query/events.cljc
old_string: (rf/reg-event-fx
  :re-frame.query/ensure-query
  (fn [{:keys [db]} [_ k params opts]]
    (let [query-config (registry/get-query! k)
          _ (when (util/infinite-query? query-config)
              (throw (ex-info (str "Query " k " is an infinite query — use :re-frame.query/ensure-infinite-query instead")
                              {:key k})))
          qid (util/query-id k params)
          query (qdb/get-query db qid)
          now (util/now-ms)
          hooks (select-keys opts [:on-success :on-failure])]
      (if (and (util/stale? query now)
               (not (:fetching? query)))
        (let [refreshing? (and (= :success (:status query))
                               (some? (:data query)))]
          (merge {:db (update-in db [:re-frame.query/queries qid] util/merge-with-default
                                 {:status (if refreshing? :success :loading)
                                  :fetching? true
                                  :stale? false})}
                 (build-query-effects query-config k params hooks)))
        {:db db}))))
new_string: (rf/reg-event-fx
  :re-frame.query/ensure-query
  (fn [{:keys [db]} [_ k params opts]]
    (let [query-config (registry/get-query! k)
          _ (when (util/infinite-query? query-config)
              (throw (ex-info (str "Query " k " is an infinite query — use :re-frame.query/ensure-infinite-query instead")
                              {:key k})))
          qid (util/query-id k params)
          query (qdb/get-query db qid)
          now (util/now-ms)
          hooks (select-keys opts [:on-success :on-failure])
          start-fx (dispatch-hooks (:on-start opts) params)]
      (if (and (util/stale? query now)
               (not (:fetching? query)))
        (let [refreshing? (and (= :success (:status query))
                               (some? (:data query)))]
          (-> (merge {:db (update-in db [:re-frame.query/queries qid] util/merge-with-default
                                     {:status (if refreshing? :success :loading)
                                      :fetching? true
                                      :stale? false})
                      :fx []}
                     (build-query-effects query-config k params hooks))
              (update :fx into start-fx)))
        {:db db}))))

## Agent (2026-04-24 16:32:11)

Wait — `build-query-effects` can return either an `effect-fn` effects map (e.g. `{:http ...}`) or the raw `query-fn` result (legacy path). In the legacy path, there might not be a `:fx` key, and merging `:fx []` would overwrite if the legacy effects happen to include `:fx`. Let me think...

Looking at the current code, `(merge {:db ...} (build-query-effects ...))` — the order is `{:db ...}` first, then the built effects. If the built effects include `:fx`, merge keeps the built one. So `start-fx` needs to be appended after the merge.

My current fix starts with `{:db ... :fx []}`, merges the built effects (which may overwrite `:fx` or not include it), then `(update :fx into start-fx)`. But if the built effects include a non-`:fx` shape (like a legacy full effects map), the `:fx` from my base map survives because merge doesn't overwrite it. Then `(update :fx into start-fx)` appends correctly. Good.

But: if built effects themselves have `:fx` (e.g., if the effect-fn produces `{:fx [[...]]}`), my base `:fx []` gets overwritten by merge, and `start-fx` is then appended to the built `:fx`. That's correct behavior — it means start hooks fire alongside any other fx.

The issue: if `start-fx` is `[]` (no on-start hook), `(update :fx into [])` still forces `:fx` key to exist, potentially as `[]`, which is a minor noise. That's acceptable.

Now `refetch-query`:

  ([db query-config k params hooks]
   (let [qid (util/query-id k params)
         query (get-in db [:re-frame.query/queries qid])
         refreshing? (and (= :success (:status query))
                          (some? (:data query)))]
     (merge {:db (update-in db [:re-frame.query/queries qid] merge
                            {:status (if refreshing? :success :loading)
                             :fetching? true
                             :stale? false})}
            (build-query-effects query-config k params hooks)))))

(rf/reg-event-fx
  :re-frame.query/refetch-query
  (fn [{:keys [db]} [_ k params hooks]]
    (let [query-config (registry/get-query! k)]
      (refetch-effects db query-config k params hooks))))
new_string: (defn- refetch-effects
  "Build the effects map for refetching a query. Shared by refetch-query
   and poll-refetch."
  ([db query-config k params]
   (refetch-effects db query-config k params nil))
  ([db query-config k params opts]
   (let [qid (util/query-id k params)
         query (get-in db [:re-frame.query/queries qid])
         refreshing? (and (= :success (:status query))
                          (some? (:data query)))
         hooks (select-keys opts [:on-success :on-failure])
         start-fx (dispatch-hooks (:on-start opts) params)]
     (-> (merge {:db (update-in db [:re-frame.query/queries qid] merge
                                {:status (if refreshing? :success :loading)
                                 :fetching? true
                                 :stale? false})
                 :fx []}
                (build-query-effects query-config k params hooks))
         (update :fx into start-fx)))))

(rf/reg-event-fx
  :re-frame.query/refetch-query
  (fn [{:keys [db]} [_ k params opts]]
    (let [query-config (registry/get-query! k)]
      (refetch-effects db query-config k params opts))))

  (testing "ensure-query fires on-start before the fetch, then on-success with data"
    (rf-test/run-test-sync

               {:hook :on-success :params {:page 1} :data [{:id 1 :title "Dune"}]}]
              @calls)
           "on-start fires first with params, then on-success with params and data")))))

## Agent (2026-04-24 16:34:01)

Wait — down from 485 to 483 assertions. That's because the old tests had 2 separate `is` checks per test, and the new version has 1 `is` for the full sequence. But — I want to double-check the ordering is actually what I'm asserting, because `rf-test/run-test-sync` processes events synchronously and the order depends on how `:fx` entries interleave.

Let me trace through `ensure-query`:
1. Handler returns `{:db ... :fx [start-fx-entry, ... ]}`
2. re-frame processes `:fx` in order, each entry is `[:dispatch [event]]`
3. `start-fx` is `[[:dispatch [:test/on-start {:page 1}]]]`
4. The effect-fn produces `:test/fake-http-success`, which in my merge order comes *before* the `start-fx` appended via `(update :fx into start-fx)`.

So the actual order of `:fx` processing:
- First: the built effects map merges into base. If effect-fn returned `{:test/fake-http-success {...}}`, that becomes a key in the effects map, not in `:fx`. 
- Then `(update :fx into start-fx)` appends start-fx to `:fx`.

re-frame processes effects in a defined order — `:db` first, then others. `:fx` is processed by iterating entries. The `:test/fake-http-success` effect (a separate top-level key) fires *synchronously* and dispatches on-success before we even get to processing `:fx`. 

Actually in re-frame, the order of effect-fn invocation is by the built-in "effect handler registration order" — but since `:fx` is itself just a compound effect, and `:test/fake-http-success` is a sibling top-level effect, which fires first?

Looking at re-frame source: `:fx` is built-in, registered early. Custom fx like `:test/fake-http-success` is registered later. The `do-fx` interceptor iterates effects in order of... actually, it iterates `(:effects context)` as a map, order isn't guaranteed. So the ordering between `:fx` (which fires on-start dispatches) and `:test/fake-http-success` (which fires on-success) is not deterministic.

But the test passed! Let me verify — it's possible `dispatch` in `run-test-sync` queues and processes FIFO, so whatever fires first gets processed first, and both before subsequent handlers. Let me check the actual order by running:

40	  value for that key - so in the example above the effect handler for :dispatch
41	  will be given one arg `[:hello 42]`.

43	  You cannot rely on the ordering in which effects are executed, other than that
44	  `:db` is guaranteed to be executed first."
45	  (->interceptor
46	   :id :do-fx
47	   :after (fn do-fx-after
48	            [context]
49	            (trace/with-trace
50	              {:op-type :event/do-fx}
51	              (let [effects            (:effects context)
52	                    effects-without-db (dissoc effects :db)]
53	                 ;; :db effect is guaranteed to be handled before all other effects.
54	                (when-let [new-db (:db effects)]
55	                  ((get-handler kind :db false) new-db))
56	                (doseq [[effect-key effect-value] effects-without-db]
57	                  (if-let [effect-fn (get-handler kind effect-key false)]
58	                    (effect-fn effect-value)
59	                    (console :warn
60	                             "re-frame: no handler registered for effect:"
61	                             effect-key
62	                             ". Ignoring."
63	                             (when (= :event effect-key)
64	                               (str "You may be trying to return a coeffect map from an event-fx handler. "
65	                                    "See https://day8.github.io/re-frame/use-cofx-as-fx/"))))))))))
66	
67	;; -- Builtin Effect Handlers  ------------------------------------------------
68	
69	;; :dispatch-later
70	;;
71	;; `dispatch` one or more events after given delays. Expects a collection
72	;; of maps with two keys:  :`ms` and `:dispatch`
73	;;
74	;; usage:
75	;;
76	;;    {:dispatch-later [{:ms 200 :dispatch [:event-id "param"]}    ;;  in 200ms do this: (dispatch [:event-id "param"])
77	;;                      {:ms 100 :dispatch [:also :this :in :100ms]}]}
78	;;
79	;; Note: nil entries in the collection are ignored which means events can be added
80	;; conditionally:
81	;;    {:dispatch-later [ (when (> 3 5) {:ms 200 :dispatch [:conditioned-out]})
82	;;                       {:ms 100 :dispatch [:another-one]}]}
83	;;
84	(defn dispatch-later
85	  [{:keys [ms dispatch] :as effect}]
86	  (if (or (empty? dispatch) (not (number? ms)))
87	    (console :error "re-frame: ignoring bad :dispatch-later value:" effect)
88	    (set-timeout! #(router/dispatch dispatch) ms)))
89	
90	(reg-fx
91	 :dispatch-later
92	 (fn [value]
93	   (if (map? value)
94	     (dispatch-later value)
95	     (doseq [effect (remove nil? value)]
96	       (dispatch-later effect)))))
97	
98	;; :fx
99	;;
100	;; Handle one or more effects. Expects a collection of vectors (tuples) of the
101	;; form [effect-key effect-value]. `nil` entries in the collection are ignored
102	;; so effects can be added conditionally.
103	;;
104	;; usage:
105	;;
106	;; {:fx [[:dispatch [:event-id "param"]]
107	;;       nil
108	;;       [:http-xhrio {:method :post
109	;;                     ...}]]}
```
````

## Agent (2026-04-24 16:34:56)

**Critical finding: "You cannot rely on the ordering in which effects are executed, other than that `:db` is guaranteed to be executed first."** (line 43-44)

So my ordering assertion `[on-start, on-success]` is relying on undefined behavior. The `:fx` (which holds `on-start`) and `:test/fake-http-success` (which dispatches `on-success`) iterate over a map — `doseq [[effect-key effect-value] effects-without-db]` — and map iteration order isn't guaranteed.

However in ClojureScript, iteration over a small literal map often preserves insertion order (for maps with <=8 entries, `PersistentArrayMap` is used which preserves order). Since `merge` preserves order of keys... the order might be deterministic in practice but not contractually.

The right fix: **put on-start inside `:fx`**, not as a separate top-level effect, so that all dispatches flow through the same `:fx` collection which DOES process in order. Wait — that's exactly what I'm doing. The issue is that `:test/fake-http-success` is a *separate* effect key, not inside `:fx`.

Let me reconsider. The current merge produces:
```
{:db ... 
 :fx [[:dispatch [:test/on-start ...]]]
 :test/fake-http-success {:on-success [...]}}
```

`do-fx` iterates `effects-without-db`, so `:fx` and `:test/fake-http-success` could fire in either order.

**Better approach:** Put the on-start dispatch BEFORE the effect-fn's result in a single `:fx` vector. But `effect-fn` returns a full effects map (`{:test/fake-http-success ...}`), not a `:fx` entry. That's fundamental to the library design.

Hmm. Actually, re-frame's `dispatch` (via `:dispatch` fx or triggered by user effects) **queues** events into the event queue — they don't run immediately. So:

1. `do-fx` processes `effects-without-db` (order undefined):
   - If `:fx` runs first: `[:dispatch [:test/on-start ...]]` → event queued. 
   - Then `:test/fake-http-success`: dispatches on-success → event queued.
   - Queue order: [on-start, on-success] → handlers run in that order.
   - If `:test/fake-http-success` runs first: on-success queued. Then `:fx`: on-start queued. Queue order: [on-success, on-start] — wrong!

So the test will pass or fail depending on effect-map iteration order. Currently it passes — but that's fragile.

**Solution:** Dispatch `on-start` via `dispatch-sync` immediately before returning the effects map. But that violates the "events return effects as data" principle.

**Better solution:** Use `:dispatch` (a built-in fx) at the same level as `:test/fake-http-success`, so both are in `:effects` as top-level keys. But `:dispatch` only takes a single event, and we might have multiple on-start hooks...

Actually the cleanest fix: change the test assertion to not rely on ordering between `on-start` and `on-success`. Since the guarantee isn't there in re-frame, our tests shouldn't encode it. Instead, assert:
- `on-start` fired once with params
- `on-success`/`on-failure` fired once with params + data/error
- Both are present in the collected calls

Let me do that — a `set` or frequency-based assertion:

Actually, thinking more — the user wanted ordering verified. The right way to guarantee `on-start` fires first is to fire it synchronously BEFORE we return effects that will trigger on-success. Let me look at how mutations do it:## User (2026-04-24 16:35:57)

WE are already doing dispatch sync since our tests use run-test-sync and in real life, usually on-success/failure happen after wire transfer so on-start will have already execute. It is fine

```
1	# Lifecycle Hooks
2	
3	Many times, you want to hook into the mutation lifecycle, possibly to trigger effects, set some db state or whatever. Re-frame query exposes lifecycle hooks for both mutations and queries:
4	
5	## Mutation lifecycle hooks
6	
7	Pass an opts map as the third argument to `execute-mutation` to hook into the mutation lifecycle:
8	
9	```clojure
10	(rf/dispatch [::rfq/execute-mutation :todos/toggle {:id 5 :done true}
11	              {:on-start   [[:my-app/on-start-event]]
12	               :on-success [[:my-app/on-success-event]]
13	               :on-failure [[:my-app/on-failure-event]]}])
14	```
15	
16	| Hook | When | Args conj'd onto each event vector |
17	|---|---|---|
18	| `:on-start` | Before the effect fires | `params` |
19	| `:on-success` | After mutation succeeds | `params`, `response-data` |
20	| `:on-failure` | After mutation fails | `params`, `error` |
21	
22	Each hook is a vector of event vectors — all events in the vector are dispatched. Hooks are optional; omitting the opts map works exactly as before.
23	
24	### Hook Handler Signatures
25	
26	rfq **conj's its own args onto every hook event you register**. This is different from how day8/http-fx and most other re-frame HTTP effects work — those dispatch `(conj on-success-event response)`, appending only the response. rfq appends `params` **and** (for `:on-success`/`:on-failure`) the response or error.
27	
28	If you pre-bind data in the hook event vector, those values sit *before* rfq's appended args:
29	
30	```clojure
31	;; Dispatch:
32	[::rfq/execute-mutation :todos/toggle {:id 5}
33	 {:on-success [[:my/hook extra-1 extra-2]]}]
34	
35	;; Hook handler receives:
36	(fn [cofx [_ extra-1 extra-2 mutation-params response]] ...)
37	;;               ^^^^^^^ ^^^^^^^  ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^  ^^^^^^^^
38	;;               your pre-bound args  rfq args
39	```
40	
41	### Signature cheat sheet
42	
43	```clojure
44	;; No pre-bound args
45	(rf/reg-event-fx :my/on-success
46	  (fn [_ [_ params response]] ...))
47	
48	(rf/reg-event-fx :my/on-failure
49	  (fn [_ [_ params error]] ...))
50	
51	(rf/reg-event-fx :my/on-start
52	  (fn [_ [_ params]] ...))
53	
54	;; With pre-bound args (e.g. a user-supplied callback fn)
55	(rf/dispatch [::rfq/execute-mutation :todos/add {:title "x"}
56	              {:on-success [[:my/on-success some-data]]}])
57	
58	(rf/reg-event-fx :my/on-success
59	  (fn [_ [_ some-data params response]] ...))
60	```
```
````

```
62	### Why this design?
63	
64	Hooks receive `params` so they can operate on the same input the mutation ran with — essential for optimistic-update snapshots keyed by the mutation input, rollback logic, and generic analytics/toast interceptors. Making `params` implicit (always passed) keeps hook events reusable across call sites without threading mutation inputs through the event vector manually.
65	
66	## Optimistic Updates Recipe
67	
68	Use lifecycle hooks + `set-query-data` to build optimistic updates in pure re-frame:
69	
70	```clojure
71	;; 1. Register hook events — these are YOUR event handlers, not library code
72	(rf/reg-event-fx :todos/optimistic-toggle
73	  (fn [{:keys [db]} [_ {:keys [id done]}]]
74	    (let [qid  [:todos/list {}]
75	          old  (get-in db [:re-frame.query/queries qid :data])
76	          new  (mapv #(if (= (:id %) id) (assoc % :done done) %) old)]
77	      {:db       (assoc-in db [:snapshots qid] old)           ;; save snapshot
78	       :dispatch [::rfq/set-query-data :todos/list {} new]}))) ;; patch cache
79	
80	(rf/reg-event-fx :todos/rollback
81	  (fn [{:keys [db]} [_ _params _error]]
82	    (let [qid [:todos/list {}]
83	          old (get-in db [:snapshots qid])]
84	      {:db       (update db :snapshots dissoc qid)
85	       :dispatch [::rfq/set-query-data :todos/list {} old]}))) ;; restore snapshot
86	
87	;; 2. Dispatch mutation with hooks
88	(rf/dispatch [::rfq/execute-mutation :todos/toggle {:id 5 :done true}
89	              {:on-start   [[:todos/optimistic-toggle]]
90	               :on-failure [[:todos/rollback]]}])
91	```
92	
93	The checkbox toggles instantly. If the server rejects, the snapshot is restored. No library magic — just re-frame events and data.
94	
95	> **Race condition note:** If a query has active polling or an in-flight refetch, the refetch response could briefly overwrite your optimistic data before the mutation completes. In practice this race is rare and self-correcting — the mutation's `:invalidates` triggers a fresh refetch with correct server data immediately after success. If you need to guard against it, see the cancellation recipe below.
96	
97	## Advanced: Cancelling In-Flight Requests
98	
99	TanStack Query solves the optimistic update race with `cancelQueries`, which aborts in-flight HTTP requests via `AbortController`. Since re-frame-query is transport-agnostic, cancellation lives in your transport layer — not in the library. Here's the pattern:
100	
101	```clojure
102	;; 1. Store AbortControllers per query in your transport layer
103	(defonce abort-controllers (atom {}))
104	
105	(rf/reg-fx :http-xhrio
106	  (fn [{:keys [method url body on-success on-failure abort-key]}]
107	    (let [controller (js/AbortController.)
108	          signal     (.-signal controller)]
109	      (when abort-key
110	        (swap! abort-controllers assoc abort-key controller))
111	      (-> (js/fetch url (clj->js {:method  (name method)
112	                                   :headers {"Content-Type" "application/json"}
113	                                   :signal  signal
114	                                   :body    (some-> body clj->js js/JSON.stringify)}))
115	          (.then  #(when (.-ok %) ...dispatch on-success...))
116	          (.catch #(when-not (.-aborted signal)  ;; silently drop aborted requests
117	                    ...dispatch on-failure...))))))
118	
119	;; 2. Register an effect that aborts a request by key
120	(rf/reg-fx :abort-request
121	  (fn [abort-key]
122	    (when-let [controller (get @abort-controllers abort-key)]
123	      (.abort controller)
124	      (swap! abort-controllers dissoc abort-key))))
125	
126	;; 3. Tag queries with an abort-key so they can be cancelled
127	(rfq/reg-query :todos/list
128	  {:query-fn (fn [_] {:method :get :url "/api/todos"
129	                       :abort-key [:todos/list {}]})
130	   :tags     (constantly [[:todos]])})
131	
132	;; 4. In your on-start hook, abort the in-flight refetch before patching
133	(rf/reg-event-fx :todos/optimistic-toggle
134	  (fn [{:keys [db]} [_ {:keys [id done]}]]
135	    (let [qid [:todos/list {}]
136	          old (get-in db [:re-frame.query/queries qid :data])
137	          new (mapv #(if (= (:id %) id) (assoc % :done done) %) old)]
138	      {:db            (assoc-in db [:snapshots qid] old)
139	       :abort-request qid                                  ;; cancel in-flight refetch
140	       :dispatch      [::rfq/set-query-data :todos/list {} new]})))
141	```
142	
143	The aborted fetch silently drops (no `on-failure` dispatch), the optimistic data stays intact, and the mutation's `:invalidates` triggers a correct refetch when the server responds.
144	
145	## Query Lifecycle Hooks
146	
147	Query lifecycles are fairly similar to the mutation ones.
148	
```
````

## Agent (2026-04-24 16:38:55)

Now I'll write the query section. It should mirror the mutation section's structure but highlight three distinct callsite patterns:

1. **`::rfq/ensure-query`** — low-level event dispatch (similar to `execute-mutation`)
2. **`::rfq/refetch-query`** — for forced refetches
3. **`::rfq/query` subscription with opts** — the idiomatic path for components

Let me also verify `on-start` behavior one more time — it fires only when a fetch actually starts (cache miss / stale). If a subscription re-runs with cached data, `ensure-query` no-ops and no hook fires. That's worth documenting.

### Tool Call [completed]: Edit docs/mutation-hooks.md

**Tool:** edit
**Timestamp:** 2026-04-24 16:39:28
**Parameters:**
file_path: /home/ovistoica/workspace/re-frame-query/docs/mutation-hooks.md
old_string: ## Query Lifecycle Hooks

Query lifecycles are fairly similar to the mutation ones.

new_string: ## Query Lifecycle Hooks

Queries support the same three hooks as mutations — `:on-start`, `:on-success`, `:on-failure` — with one important difference: **hooks only fire when a fetch actually happens.** If `ensure-query` is called and the cache is fresh (not stale, not fetching), no hooks fire because there is no fetch lifecycle to observe.

| Hook | When | Args conj'd onto each event vector |
|---|---|---|
| `:on-start` | Before the HTTP effect fires, once the query is committed to `:loading`/`:fetching?` | `params` |
| `:on-success` | After the query succeeds (receives post-`:transform-response` data) | `params`, `data` |
| `:on-failure` | After the query fails (receives post-`:transform-error` error) | `params`, `error` |

Same handler-signature rules as mutations — rfq conj's `params` and the response/error onto every hook event vector you register. Pre-bound args sit before rfq's args:

```clojure
(rf/reg-event-fx :my/on-success
  (fn [_ [_ params data]] ...))

(rf/reg-event-fx :my/on-failure
  (fn [_ [_ params error]] ...))

(rf/reg-event-fx :my/on-start
  (fn [_ [_ params]] ...))
```

### Three ways to pass hooks

#### 1. Via `::rfq/ensure-query` (from an event handler)

Use this when you're kicking off a query from code — e.g. from a route-change event, a button-click handler, or a prefetch. Pass the hooks as an opts map in the fourth position:

```clojure
(rf/reg-event-fx :route/books-page-entered
  (fn [_ [_ page]]
    {:dispatch [::rfq/ensure-query :books/list {:page page}
                {:on-start   [[:analytics/track :books-fetch-started]]
                 :on-success [[:analytics/track :books-fetch-succeeded]]
                 :on-failure [[:toast/error "Could not load books"]]}]}))
```

Hooks thread through the full chain: `ensure-query` → your effect adapter → `query-success`/`query-failure` → dispatched to your handlers.

#### 2. Via `::rfq/refetch-query`

Same API, but forces a fetch regardless of staleness. Useful for "pull-to-refresh" or manual retry:

```clojure
(rf/reg-event-fx :books/retry-after-error
  (fn [_ [_ page]]
    {:dispatch [::rfq/refetch-query :books/list {:page page}
                {:on-success [[:toast/success "Books loaded"]]
                 :on-failure [[:toast/error "Still failing — try later"]]}]}))
```

#### 3. Via the `::rfq/query` subscription

Pass the opts map as the fourth arg to `subscribe`. The subscription forwards hooks to `ensure-query` on first mount:

```clojure
(defn books-list [page]
  (let [q @(rf/subscribe [::rfq/query :books/list {:page page}
                          {:on-success [[:analytics/track :books-loaded-from-view]]
                           :on-failure [[:toast/error "Could not load books"]]}])]
    [:div
     (case (:status q)
       :loading [:p "Loading..."]
       :error   [:p "Something went wrong"]
       :success [book-items (:data q)])]))
```

Hook events are **not** wired to the subscription lifecycle — they fire exactly once per fetch, not on every render. Re-subscriptions with the same `k`/`params` deduplicate through the cache: if the data is fresh, no fetch happens and no hooks fire.

> **Scope tip:** if two components subscribe to the same query with different hooks, only the first subscription's opts win the race on the initial mount, and neither runs on cache hits. For reliable cross-component observability, prefer registering hooks from a single coordinating event handler and using plain `::rfq/query-data` / `::rfq/query-status` in views.

### When each hook fires

```
┌────────────────┐  :on-start fires here
│ ensure-query   │──────────┐
│ (cache miss or │          ▼
│  stale + no    │  ┌──────────────┐  effect resolves
│  in-flight)    │  │  HTTP effect │──────┐
└────────────────┘  └──────────────┘      ▼
                                    ┌───────────────┐  :on-success / :on-failure
                                    │ query-success │  fires here (after :data is
                                    │ query-failure │  committed to app-db)
                                    └───────────────┘
```

Because `:on-success` fires **after** the `:db` effect commits, your hook handlers can read the fresh data from `app-db` directly — for example, to patch a denormalized view or seed a dependent query:

```clojure
(rf/reg-event-fx :books/sync-count
  (fn [{:keys [db]} [_ _params data]]
    {:dispatch [::rfq/set-query-data :books/count {} {:count (count data)}]}))

;; Register the hook when subscribing or in an init event:
(rf/dispatch [::rfq/ensure-query :books/list {}
              {:on-success [[:books/sync-count]]}])
```

### Important: polling and tag-invalidation refetches don't carry hooks

Hooks are a **caller contract** — they belong to the specific `ensure-query`/`refetch-query`/`subscribe` call that declared them. The internal refetches triggered by:

- `:polling-interval-ms` (automatic polling)
- `::rfq/invalidate-tags` → active-query refetch

...do **not** fire your hooks, because the library can't know whose opts to carry. If you need lifecycle observability for those paths, register a global re-frame interceptor on `:re-frame.query/query-success` / `:re-frame.query/query-failure` events instead.


````
╭─────────╮
│ changes │
╰─────────╯

@@ -1,4 +1,108 @@
 
 ## Query Lifecycle Hooks
 
-Query lifecycles are fairly similar to the mutation ones.
\ No newline at end of file
+Queries support the same three hooks as mutations — `:on-start`, `:on-success`, `:on-failure` — with one important difference: **hooks only fire when a fetch actually happens.** If `ensure-query` is called and the cache is fresh (not stale, not fetching), no hooks fire because there is no fetch lifecycle to observe.
+
+| Hook | When | Args conj'd onto each event vector |
+|---|---|---|
+| `:on-start` | Before the HTTP effect fires, once the query is committed to `:loading`/`:fetching?` | `params` |
+| `:on-success` | After the query succeeds (receives post-`:transform-response` data) | `params`, `data` |
+| `:on-failure` | After the query fails (receives post-`:transform-error` error) | `params`, `error` |
+
+Same handler-signature rules as mutations — rfq conj's `params` and the response/error onto every hook event vector you register. Pre-bound args sit before rfq's args:
+
+```clojure
+(rf/reg-event-fx :my/on-success
+  (fn [_ [_ params data]] ...))
+
+(rf/reg-event-fx :my/on-failure
+  (fn [_ [_ params error]] ...))
+
+(rf/reg-event-fx :my/on-start
+  (fn [_ [_ params]] ...))
+```
+
+### Three ways to pass hooks
+
+#### 1. Via `::rfq/ensure-query` (from an event handler)
+
+Use this when you're kicking off a query from code — e.g. from a route-change event, a button-click handler, or a prefetch. Pass the hooks as an opts map in the fourth position:
+
+```clojure
+(rf/reg-event-fx :route/books-page-entered
+  (fn [_ [_ page]]
+    {:dispatch [::rfq/ensure-query :books/list {:page page}
+                {:on-start   [[:analytics/track :books-fetch-started]]
+                 :on-success [[:analytics/track :books-fetch-succeeded]]
+                 :on-failure [[:toast/error "Could not load books"]]}]}))
+```
+
+Hooks thread through the full chain: `ensure-query` → your effect adapter → `query-success`/`query-failure` → dispatched to your handlers.
+
+#### 2. Via `::rfq/refetch-query`
+
+Same API, but forces a fetch regardless of staleness. Useful for "pull-to-refresh" or manual retry:
+
+```clojure
+(rf/reg-event-fx :books/retry-after-error
+  (fn [_ [_ page]]
+    {:dispatch [::rfq/refetch-query :books/list {:page page}
+                {:on-success [[:toast/success "Books loaded"]]
+                 :on-failure [[:toast/error "Still failing — try later"]]}]}))
+```
+
+#### 3. Via the `::rfq/query` subscription
+
+Pass the opts map as the fourth arg to `subscribe`. The subscription forwards hooks to `ensure-query` on first mount:
+
+```clojure
+(defn books-list [page]
+  (let [q @(rf/subscribe [::rfq/query :books/list {:page page}
+                          {:on-success [[:analytics/track :books-loaded-from-view]]
+                           :on-failure [[:toast/error "Could not load books"]]}])]
+    [:div
+     (case (:status q)
+       :loading [:p "Loading..."]
+       :error   [:p "Something went wrong"]
+       :success [book-items (:data q)])]))
+```
+
+Hook events are **not** wired to the subscription lifecycle — they fire exactly once per fetch, not on every render. Re-subscriptions with the same `k`/`params` deduplicate through the cache: if the data is fresh, no fetch happens and no hooks fire.
+
+> **Scope tip:** if two components subscribe to the same query with different hooks, only the first subscription's opts win the race on the initial mount, and neither runs on cache hits. For reliable cross-component observability, prefer registering hooks from a single coordinating event handler and using plain `::rfq/query-data` / `::rfq/query-status` in views.
+
+### When each hook fires
+
+```
+┌────────────────┐  :on-start fires here
+│ ensure-query   │──────────┐
+│ (cache miss or │          ▼
+│  stale + no    │  ┌──────────────┐  effect resolves
+│  in-flight)    │  │  HTTP effect │──────┐
+└────────────────┘  └──────────────┘      ▼
+                                    ┌───────────────┐  :on-success / :on-failure
+                                    │ query-success │  fires here (after :data is
+                                    │ query-failure │  committed to app-db)
+                                    └───────────────┘
+```
+
+Because `:on-success` fires **after** the `:db` effect commits, your hook handlers can read the fresh data from `app-db` directly — for example, to patch a denormalized view or seed a dependent query:
+
+```clojure
+(rf/reg-event-fx :books/sync-count
+  (fn [{:keys [db]} [_ _params data]]
+    {:dispatch [::rfq/set-query-data :books/count {} {:count (count data)}]}))
+
+;; Register the hook when subscribing or in an init event:
+(rf/dispatch [::rfq/ensure-query :books/list {}
+              {:on-success [[:books/sync-count]]}])
+```
+
+### Important: polling and tag-invalidation refetches don't carry hooks
+
+Hooks are a **caller contract** — they belong to the specific `ensure-query`/`refetch-query`/`subscribe` call that declared them. The internal refetches triggered by:
+
+- `:polling-interval-ms` (automatic polling)
+- `::rfq/invalidate-tags` → active-query refetch
+
+...do **not** fire your hooks, because the library can't know whose opts to carry. If you need lifecycle observability for those paths, register a global re-frame interceptor on `:re-frame.query/query-success` / `:re-frame.query/query-failure` events instead.
\ No newline at end of file
````

145	## Query Lifecycle Hooks
146	
147	Queries support the same three hooks as mutations — `:on-start`, `:on-success`, `:on-failure` — with one important difference: **hooks only fire when a fetch actually happens.** If `ensure-query` is called and the cache is fresh (not stale, not fetching), no hooks fire because there is no fetch lifecycle to observe.
148	
149	| Hook | When | Args conj'd onto each event vector |
150	|---|---|---|
151	| `:on-start` | Before the HTTP effect fires, once the query is committed to `:loading`/`:fetching?` | `params` |
152	| `:on-success` | After the query succeeds (receives post-`:transform-response` data) | `params`, `data` |
153	| `:on-failure` | After the query fails (receives post-`:transform-error` error) | `params`, `error` |
154	
155	Same handler-signature rules as mutations — rfq conj's `params` and the response/error onto every hook event vector you register. Pre-bound args sit before rfq's args:
156	
157	```clojure
158	(rf/reg-event-fx :my/on-success
159	  (fn [_ [_ params data]] ...))
160	
161	(rf/reg-event-fx :my/on-failure
162	  (fn [_ [_ params error]] ...))
163	
164	(rf/reg-event-fx :my/on-start
165	  (fn [_ [_ params]] ...))
166	```
167	
168	### Three ways to pass hooks
169	
170	#### 1. Via `::rfq/ensure-query` (from an event handler)
171	
172	Use this when you're kicking off a query from code — e.g. from a route-change event, a button-click handler, or a prefetch. Pass the hooks as an opts map in the fourth position:
173	
174	```clojure
175	(rf/reg-event-fx :route/books-page-entered
176	  (fn [_ [_ page]]
177	    {:dispatch [::rfq/ensure-query :books/list {:page page}
178	                {:on-start   [[:analytics/track :books-fetch-started]]
179	                 :on-success [[:analytics/track :books-fetch-succeeded]]
180	                 :on-failure [[:toast/error "Could not load books"]]}]}))
181	```
182	
183	Hooks thread through the full chain: `ensure-query` → your effect adapter → `query-success`/`query-failure` → dispatched to your handlers.
184	
185	#### 2. Via `::rfq/refetch-query`
186	
187	Same API, but forces a fetch regardless of staleness. Useful for "pull-to-refresh" or manual retry:
188	
189	```clojure
190	(rf/reg-event-fx :books/retry-after-error
191	  (fn [_ [_ page]]
192	    {:dispatch [::rfq/refetch-query :books/list {:page page}
193	                {:on-success [[:toast/success "Books loaded"]]
194	                 :on-failure [[:toast/error "Still failing — try later"]]}]}))
195	```
196	
197	#### 3. Via the `::rfq/query` subscription
198	
199	Pass the opts map as the fourth arg to `subscribe`. The subscription forwards hooks to `ensure-query` on first mount:
200	
201	```clojure
202	(defn books-list [page]
203	  (let [q @(rf/subscribe [::rfq/query :books/list {:page page}
204	                          {:on-success [[:analytics/track :books-loaded-from-view]]
205	                           :on-failure [[:toast/error "Could not load books"]]}])]
206	    [:div
207	     (case (:status q)
208	       :loading [:p "Loading..."]
209	       :error   [:p "Something went wrong"]
210	       :success [book-items (:data q)])]))
211	```
212	
213	Hook events are **not** wired to the subscription lifecycle — they fire exactly once per fetch, not on every render. Re-subscriptions with the same `k`/`params` deduplicate through the cache: if the data is fresh, no fetch happens and no hooks fire.
214	
215	> **Scope tip:** if two components subscribe to the same query with different hooks, only the first subscription's opts win the race on the initial mount, and neither runs on cache hits. For reliable cross-component observability, prefer registering hooks from a single coordinating event handler and using plain `::rfq/query-data` / `::rfq/query-status` in views.
216	
217	### When each hook fires
218	
219	```
220	┌────────────────┐  :on-start fires here
221	│ ensure-query   │──────────┐
222	│ (cache miss or │          ▼
223	│  stale + no    │  ┌──────────────┐  effect resolves
224	│  in-flight)    │  │  HTTP effect │──────┐
225	└────────────────┘  └──────────────┘      ▼
226	                                    ┌───────────────┐  :on-success / :on-failure
227	                                    │ query-success │  fires here (after :data is
228	                                    │ query-failure │  committed to app-db)
229	                                    └───────────────┘
230	```
231	
232	Because `:on-success` fires **after** the `:db` effect commits, your hook handlers can read the fresh data from `app-db` directly — for example, to patch a denormalized view or seed a dependent query:
233

236	  (fn [{:keys [db]} [_ _params data]]
237	    {:dispatch [::rfq/set-query-data :books/count {} {:count (count data)}]}))

## Agent (2026-04-24 16:39:51)

## Summary

**Doc rewrite** (`docs/mutation-hooks.md:145-251`):

- Hook table with fire-timing and args conj'd
- Signature cheat sheet
- **Three call patterns**: `::rfq/ensure-query` (event), `::rfq/refetch-query` (event), `::rfq/query` (subscription with opts)
- When-each-fires diagram
- Recipe: read fresh data from `app-db` in `:on-success` to patch dependent queries
- **Important caveat** documented: hooks don't fire from polling or `invalidate-tags` refetches (caller contract limitation), with the escape hatch pointer to global interceptors

**Small library fix** found and corrected while researching for the doc: `subs.cljc:73` had `(select-keys opts [:on-success :on-success])` (duplicate key, missing `:on-start` and `:on-failure`). Now correctly selects `[:on-start :on-success :on-failure]` so the subscription path actually threads hooks through to `ensure-query`.

109 tests / 483 assertions still passing.

```
60	| `[::rfq/ensure-query k params]` | Fetch if stale/absent (called automatically by subscription; can also used for prefetching) |
61	| `[::rfq/refetch-query k params]` | Force refetch regardless of staleness |
62	| `[::rfq/execute-mutation k params]` | Execute a mutation |
63	| `[::rfq/execute-mutation k params opts]` | Execute with [lifecycle hooks](mutation-hooks.md) |
64	| `[::rfq/set-query-data k params data]` | Directly set cached query data (for optimistic updates, rollback) |
65	| `[::rfq/invalidate-tags tags]` | Mark matching queries stale & refetch active ones |
66	| `[::rfq/remove-query qid]` | Remove a specific query from cache (used internally by GC) |
67	| `[::rfq/garbage-collect]` | Bulk remove all expired inactive queries |

### Tool Call [completed]: Read skill/re-frame-query/SKILL.md (155 - 159)

**Tool:** read
**Timestamp:** 2026-04-24 16:40:25
**Parameters:**
file_path: /home/ovistoica/workspace/re-frame-query/skill/re-frame-query/SKILL.md
offset: 155
limit: 5

````
```
155	day8/http-fx dispatches `(conj on-success response)` — only the response is appended. rfq dispatches `(conj ev params data)` — **both** params and response are appended. A handler copied verbatim from an http-fx callback will silently bind the rfq mutation-params map to the `response` slot and drop the real response. Symptoms: `(:some-key response)` returns `nil`, downstream `assoc-in` paths collapse to `[... nil ...]`, and `app-db` gets corrupted at an unexpected branch. Always update the signature to include `mutation-params` between any pre-bound args and the response.
156	
157	See [docs/mutation-hooks.md](../../docs/mutation-hooks.md) for the full pattern with worked examples.
158	
159	## Optimistic Updates
```
````

 | [Prefetching](docs/prefetching.md) | Pre-populate cache on hover or route transition |

 | [Effect Overrides](docs/effect-overrides.md) | Per-query transport, custom callbacks |
-| [Mutation Hooks](docs/mutation-hooks.md) | Lifecycle hooks, optimistic updates, request cancellation |
+| [Lifecycle Hooks](docs/lifecycle-hooks.md) | Query & mutation hooks, optimistic updates, request cancellation |
 | [Infinite Queries](docs/infinite-queries.md) | Cursor-based pagination, sequential re-fetch, sliding window |
 
 ## How It Works
\ No newline at end of file
```

 | `[::rfq/set-query-data k params data]` | Directly set cached query data (for optimistic updates, rollback) |
 | `[::rfq/invalidate-tags tags]` | Mark matching queries stale & refetch active ones |
 | `[::rfq/remove-query qid]` | Remove a specific query from cache (used internally by GC) |

╭─────────╮
│ changes │
╰─────────╯

@@ -1,6 +1,6 @@
 
 day8/http-fx dispatches `(conj on-success response)` — only the response is appended. rfq dispatches `(conj ev params data)` — **both** params and response are appended. A handler copied verbatim from an http-fx callback will silently bind the rfq mutation-params map to the `response` slot and drop the real response. Symptoms: `(:some-key response)` returns `nil`, downstream `assoc-in` paths collapse to `[... nil ...]`, and `app-db` gets corrupted at an unexpected branch. Always update the signature to include `mutation-params` between any pre-bound args and the response.
 
-See [docs/mutation-hooks.md](../../docs/mutation-hooks.md) for the full pattern with worked examples.
+See [docs/lifecycle-hooks.md](../../docs/lifecycle-hooks.md) for the full pattern with worked examples.
 
 ## Optimistic Updates
```

### Tool Call [failed]: Edit docs/lifecycle-hooks.md

**Tool:** edit
**Timestamp:** 2026-04-24 16:45:06
**Parameters:**
file_path: /home/ovistoica/workspace/re-frame-query/docs/lifecycle-hooks.md
old_string: ### Three ways to pass hooks

#### 1. Via `::rfq/ensure-query` (from an event handler)

Use this when you're kicking off a query from code — e.g. from a route-change event, a button-click handler, or a prefetch. Pass the hooks as an opts map in the fourth position:

```clojure
(rf/reg-event-fx :route/books-page-entered
  (fn [_ [_ page]]
    {:dispatch [::rfq/ensure-query :books/list {:page page}
                {:on-start   [[:analytics/track :books-fetch-started]]
                 :on-success [[:analytics/track :books-fetch-succeeded]]
                 :on-failure [[:toast/error "Could not load books"]]}]}))
```

Hooks thread through the full chain: `ensure-query` → your effect adapter → `query-success`/`query-failure` → dispatched to your handlers.

#### 2. Via `::rfq/refetch-query`

Same API, but forces a fetch regardless of staleness. Useful for "pull-to-refresh" or manual retry:

```clojure
(rf/reg-event-fx :books/retry-after-error
  (fn [_ [_ page]]
    {:dispatch [::rfq/refetch-query :books/list {:page page}
                {:on-success [[:toast/success "Books loaded"]]
                 :on-failure [[:toast/error "Still failing — try later"]]}]}))
```

#### 3. Via the `::rfq/query` subscription

Pass the opts map as the fourth arg to `subscribe`. The subscription forwards hooks to `ensure-query` on first mount:

```clojure
(defn books-list [page]
  (let [q @(rf/subscribe [::rfq/query :books/list {:page page}
                          {:on-success [[:analytics/track :books-loaded-from-view]]
                           :on-failure [[:toast/error "Could not load books"]]}])]
    [:div
     (case (:status q)
       :loading [:p "Loading..."]
       :error   [:p "Something went wrong"]
       :success [book-items (:data q)])]))
```

Hook events are **not** wired to the subscription lifecycle — they fire exactly once per fetch, not on every render. Re-subscriptions with the same `k`/`params` deduplicate through the cache: if the data is fresh, no fetch happens and no hooks fire.

> **Scope tip:** if two components subscribe to the same query with different hooks, only the first subscription's opts win the race on the initial mount, and neither runs on cache hits. For reliable cross-component observability, prefer registering hooks from a single coordinating event handler and using plain `::rfq/query-data` / `::rfq/query-status` in views.
new_string: ### Recommended: trigger queries from navigation events

The most idiomatic place to wire hooks is a route-enter event that explicitly dispatches `ensure-query` with its opts. This keeps the lifecycle deterministic — the fetch starts exactly once, exactly when you intend, and the hooks fire in-band with navigation. Combine it with `::rfq/query-state` (passive subscription) in the view so the component only reads data, never triggers fetches.

```clojure
;; On route enter — fire the fetch with hooks, then mark-active for polling/GC
(rf/dispatch [::rfq/ensure-query :books/list {:page 1}
              {:on-start   [[:analytics/track :books-fetch-started]]
               :on-success [[:analytics/track :books-fetch-succeeded]]
               :on-failure [[:toast/error "Could not load books"]]}])
(rf/dispatch [::rfq/mark-active :books/list {:page 1}])

;; In views — passive read, no side effects
(let [{:keys [status data]} @(rf/subscribe [::rfq/query-state :books/list {:page 1}])]
  ...)

;; On route leave
(rf/dispatch [::rfq/mark-inactive :books/list {:page 1}])
```

See the [polling guide](polling.md) for the full route enter/leave pattern with `mark-active` / `mark-inactive` — the same structure applies whether or not you're polling.

### Forcing a fetch: `::rfq/refetch-query`

Same API, but ignores staleness. Good for pull-to-refresh or retry-after-error:

```clojure
(rf/dispatch [::rfq/refetch-query :books/list {:page 1}
              {:on-success [[:toast/success "Books loaded"]]
               :on-failure [[:toast/error "Still failing — try later"]]}])
```

### Hooks via the `::rfq/query` subscription (best-effort)

You can also pass hooks as the fourth arg to `::rfq/query`:

```clojure
@(rf/subscribe [::rfq/query :books/list {:page 1}
                {:on-success [[:analytics/track :books-loaded-from-view]]
                 :on-failure [[:toast/error "Could not load books"]]}])
```

**This is convenient but not reliable.** The subscription only triggers `ensure-query` on first mount, so hooks fire only if that call actually starts a fetch. Any of these scenarios will cause hooks to silently not run:

- The cache is already fresh (stale-while-revalidate no-op).
- Another component already mounted the same query first (dedup).
- The component mounts, reads fresh data, and unmounts before the data goes stale.

For anything you actually care about — analytics, toasts, post-success cache patching — trigger the fetch from a navigation event and keep the view using `::rfq/query-state` or `::rfq/query-data`. The subscription-opts path is fine for local, best-effort concerns (e.g. a debug log) where missing a fire is harmless.

````
```

new_string: ### Recommended: trigger queries from navigation events

The most idiomatic place to wire hooks is a route-enter event that explicitly dispatches `ensure-query` with its opts. This keeps the lifecycle deterministic — the fetch starts exactly once, exactly when you intend, and the hooks fire in-band with navigation. Combine it with `::rfq/query-state` (passive subscription) in the view so the component only reads data, never triggers fetches.

```clojure
;; On route enter — fire the fetch with hooks, then mark-active for polling/GC
(rf/dispatch [::rfq/ensure-query :books/list {:page 1}
              {:on-start   [[:analytics/track :books-fetch-started]]
               :on-success [[:analytics/track :books-fetch-succeeded]]
               :on-failure [[:toast/error "Could not load books"]]}])
(rf/dispatch [::rfq/mark-active :books/list {:page 1}])

;; In views — passive read, no side effects
(let [{:keys [status data]} @(rf/subscribe [::rfq/query-state :books/list {:page 1}])]
  ...)

;; On route leave
(rf/dispatch [::rfq/mark-inactive :books/list {:page 1}])
```

See the [polling guide](polling.md) for the full route enter/leave pattern with `mark-active` / `mark-inactive` — the same structure applies whether or not you're polling.

### Forcing a fetch: `::rfq/refetch-query`

Same API, but ignores staleness. Good for pull-to-refresh or retry-after-error:

```clojure
(rf/dispatch [::rfq/refetch-query :books/list {:page 1}
              {:on-success [[:toast/success "Books loaded"]]
               :on-failure [[:toast/error "Still failing — try later"]]}])
```

### Hooks via the `::rfq/query` subscription (best-effort)

You can also pass hooks as the fourth arg to `::rfq/query`:

```clojure
@(rf/subscribe [::rfq/query :books/list {:page 1}
                {:on-success [[:analytics/track :books-loaded-from-view]]
                 :on-failure [[:toast/error "Could not load books"]]}])
```

**This is convenient but not reliable.** The subscription only triggers `ensure-query` on first mount, so hooks fire only if that call actually starts a fetch. Any of these scenarios will cause hooks to silently not run:

- The cache is already fresh (stale-while-revalidate no-op).
- Another component already mounted the same query first (dedup).
- The component mounts, reads fresh data, and unmounts before the data goes stale.

For anything you actually care about — analytics, toasts, post-success cache patching — trigger the fetch from a navigation event and keep the view using `::rfq/query-state` or `::rfq/query-data`. The subscription-opts path is fine for local, best-effort concerns (e.g. a debug log) where missing a fire is harmless.

````
╭─────────╮
│ changes │
╰─────────╯

@@ -1,54 +1,55 @@
   (fn [_ [_ params]] ...))
 ```
 
-### Three ways to pass hooks
+### Recommended: trigger queries from navigation events
 
-#### 1. Via `::rfq/ensure-query` (from an event handler)
-
-Use this when you're kicking off a query from code — e.g. from a route-change event, a button-click handler, or a prefetch. Pass the hooks as an opts map in the fourth position:
+The most idiomatic place to wire hooks is a route-enter event that explicitly dispatches `ensure-query` with its opts. This keeps the lifecycle deterministic — the fetch starts exactly once, exactly when you intend, and the hooks fire in-band with navigation. Combine it with `::rfq/query-state` (passive subscription) in the view so the component only reads data, never triggers fetches.
 
 ```clojure
-(rf/reg-event-fx :route/books-page-entered
-  (fn [_ [_ page]]
-    {:dispatch [::rfq/ensure-query :books/list {:page page}
-                {:on-start   [[:analytics/track :books-fetch-started]]
-                 :on-success [[:analytics/track :books-fetch-succeeded]]
-                 :on-failure [[:toast/error "Could not load books"]]}]}))
+;; On route enter — fire the fetch with hooks, then mark-active for polling/GC
+(rf/dispatch [::rfq/ensure-query :books/list {:page 1}
+              {:on-start   [[:analytics/track :books-fetch-started]]
+               :on-success [[:analytics/track :books-fetch-succeeded]]
+               :on-failure [[:toast/error "Could not load books"]]}])
+(rf/dispatch [::rfq/mark-active :books/list {:page 1}])
+
+;; In views — passive read, no side effects
+(let [{:keys [status data]} @(rf/subscribe [::rfq/query-state :books/list {:page 1}])]
+  ...)
+
+;; On route leave
+(rf/dispatch [::rfq/mark-inactive :books/list {:page 1}])
 ```
 
-Hooks thread through the full chain: `ensure-query` → your effect adapter → `query-success`/`query-failure` → dispatched to your handlers.
+See the [polling guide](polling.md) for the full route enter/leave pattern with `mark-active` / `mark-inactive` — the same structure applies whether or not you're polling.
 
-#### 2. Via `::rfq/refetch-query`
+### Forcing a fetch: `::rfq/refetch-query`
 
-Same API, but forces a fetch regardless of staleness. Useful for "pull-to-refresh" or manual retry:
+Same API, but ignores staleness. Good for pull-to-refresh or retry-after-error:
 
 ```clojure
-(rf/reg-event-fx :books/retry-after-error
-  (fn [_ [_ page]]
-    {:dispatch [::rfq/refetch-query :books/list {:page page}
-                {:on-success [[:toast/success "Books loaded"]]
-                 :on-failure [[:toast/error "Still failing — try later"]]}]}))
+(rf/dispatch [::rfq/refetch-query :books/list {:page 1}
+              {:on-success [[:toast/success "Books loaded"]]
+               :on-failure [[:toast/error "Still failing — try later"]]}])
 ```
 
-#### 3. Via the `::rfq/query` subscription
+### Hooks via the `::rfq/query` subscription (best-effort)
 
-Pass the opts map as the fourth arg to `subscribe`. The subscription forwards hooks to `ensure-query` on first mount:
+You can also pass hooks as the fourth arg to `::rfq/query`:
 
 ```clojure
-(defn books-list [page]
-  (let [q @(rf/subscribe [::rfq/query :books/list {:page page}
-                          {:on-success [[:analytics/track :books-loaded-from-view]]
-                           :on-failure [[:toast/error "Could not load books"]]}])]
-    [:div
-     (case (:status q)
-       :loading [:p "Loading..."]
-       :error   [:p "Something went wrong"]
-       :success [book-items (:data q)])]))
+@(rf/subscribe [::rfq/query :books/list {:page 1}
+                {:on-success [[:analytics/track :books-loaded-from-view]]
+                 :on-failure [[:toast/error "Could not load books"]]}])
 ```
 
-Hook events are **not** wired to the subscription lifecycle — they fire exactly once per fetch, not on every render. Re-subscriptions with the same `k`/`params` deduplicate through the cache: if the data is fresh, no fetch happens and no hooks fire.
+**This is convenient but not reliable.** The subscription only triggers `ensure-query` on first mount, so hooks fire only if that call actually starts a fetch. Any of these scenarios will cause hooks to silently not run:
+
+- The cache is already fresh (stale-while-revalidate no-op).
+- Another component already mounted the same query first (dedup).
+- The component mounts, reads fresh data, and unmounts before the data goes stale.
 
-> **Scope tip:** if two components subscribe to the same query with different hooks, only the first subscription's opts win the race on the initial mount, and neither runs on cache hits. For reliable cross-component observability, prefer registering hooks from a single coordinating event handler and using plain `::rfq/query-data` / `::rfq/query-status` in views.
+For anything you actually care about — analytics, toasts, post-success cache patching — trigger the fetch from a navigation event and keep the view using `::rfq/query-state` or `::rfq/query-data`. The subscription-opts path is fine for local, best-effort concerns (e.g. a debug log) where missing a fire is harmless.
 
 ### When each hook fires
````

## Agent (2026-04-24 16:45:40)

Reads cleanly. Key changes:

1. **No more `reg-event-fx` wrappers** — all dispatches are bare `rf/dispatch` calls, no double event-loop hops.
2. **Navigation events as the primary pattern** — frames `::rfq/ensure-query` + `mark-active` as the idiomatic way to wire hooks, with `::rfq/query-state` in views.
3. **Link to `polling.md`** — refers users to the full route enter/leave pattern.
4. **Subscription-opts demoted to "best-effort"** — explicitly calls out the three scenarios where hooks silently don't fire, steering readers away for anything consequential.

```
1	# Changelog
2	
3	All notable changes to this project will be documented in this file.
4	
5	The format is based on [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/).
6	
7	## [0.7.0] - 2026-04-11
8	
9	### Added
10	
11	- **`::rfq/infinite-query-data`** — passive derived subscription returning just the `:data` field of an infinite query (`{:pages [...] :has-next? bool :has-prev? bool ...}`). Components subscribed here only re-render when pages actually change, not when `:fetching-next?`, `:fetching-prev?`, or `:stale?` toggle. Useful in stale-while-revalidate patterns.
12	
13	### Fixed
14	
15	- **`has-prev?` is now always present in infinite query data** — previously `has-prev?` was only assoc'd into the data map when `:get-previous-cursor` was configured, making `(:has-prev? data)` return nil (not false) for forward-only queries. It is now always included as `false` when no previous page exists. `prev-cursor` is still only included when `:get-previous-cursor` is configured.
16	
17	## [0.6.0] - 2026-04-11
18	
19	### Fixed
20	
21	- **Derived subscriptions no longer trigger fetches** ⚠️ **Breaking change** — `::rfq/query-data`, `::rfq/query-status`, `::rfq/query-fetching?`, and `::rfq/query-error` were incorrectly depending on the effectful `::rfq/query` subscription, causing a fetch to be triggered whenever any of them were subscribed to. They now derive from the passive `::rfq/query-state`, matching their documented "no fetch" contract. If you were relying on these subscriptions to trigger fetches, replace them with `::rfq/query` or dispatch `::rfq/ensure-query` explicitly.
22	
23	## [0.5.0] - 2026-04-07
24	
25	### Added
26	
27	- **Polling via `mark-active` / `mark-inactive` events** — `mark-active` now accepts an opts map with `:polling-interval-ms` (falls back to query config) and `:sub-id` (defaults to `:default`). `mark-inactive` stops the subscriber's poll. This makes event-based lifecycle a first-class citizen for polling — no effectful subscription required.
28	- **`get-query!` / `get-mutation!` in registry** — throwing variants of `get-query`/`get-mutation` that raise `ex-info` when the key is not registered, replacing the repeated `(or (get-query k) (throw ...))` pattern.
29	
30	### Fixed
31	
32	- **Polling no longer fires duplicate requests when a fetch is in-flight** — polling now dispatches `::rfq/poll-refetch` instead of `::rfq/refetch-query`. By default, if a query is already fetching when a poll tick fires, the tick is skipped — preventing stale-response races where a late T0 response overwrites a fresher T1 response. Set `:polling-mode :force` on the query config to restore the old behavior. Manual `refetch-query` calls remain unconditional.
33	
34	### Changed
35	
36	- **Polling logic moved from subscriptions to events** — the effectful `::rfq/query` subscription no longer manages polling directly. It passes opts through to `mark-active`/`mark-inactive`, which are now the single authority for polling lifecycle.
37	- **Event handlers use `registry/get-query!`** — all event handlers now use the throwing `get-query!` / `get-mutation!` variants, removing duplicated boilerplate.
38	
39	## [0.4.0] - 2026-04-03
40	
41	### Added
42	
43	- **`re-frame.query.db` namespace** — new namespace of pure `db → db` functions for reading and transforming the query cache inline inside your own event handlers, without dispatching extra events. Import as `[re-frame.query.db :as rfq-db]`.
44	  - `get-query [db k params]` — full cache entry
45	  - `get-query-data [db k params]` — just `:data`
46	  - `set-query-data [db k params data]` — write to cache (`:success`, fresh)
47	  - `remove-query [db qid]` — evict one inactive query
48	  - `garbage-collect [db]` / `[db now]` — bulk eviction of expired inactive queries
49	
50	### Changed
51	
52	- **Event handlers delegate to `re-frame.query.db`** — the `set-query-data`, `remove-query`, and `garbage-collect` event handlers now call the pure db functions directly, eliminating duplicated logic.
53	
54	## [0.3.1] - 2026-04-03
55	
56	### Changed
57	
58	- **`enable-debug-logging!` accepts options map** — pass `{:clj->js? false}` to skip `clj->js` conversion and log raw ClojureScript values instead. Useful when Chrome custom formatters (e.g. cljs-devtools) are enabled. Default behaviour (`clj->js? true`) is unchanged.
59	
60	## [0.3.0] - 2026-04-02
```
````

**Parameters:**

## [0.7.0] - 2026-04-11
new_string: The format is based on [Keep a Changelog](https://keepachangelog.com/en/1.1.0/).

## [Unreleased]

### Added

- **Query lifecycle hooks** — `:on-start`, `:on-success`, `:on-failure` for standard queries, mirroring the mutation hooks API. Pass them as the opts map to `::rfq/ensure-query`, `::rfq/refetch-query`, or as the fourth arg to an `::rfq/query` subscription. Hooks fire only when a fetch actually starts (not on cache hits) and are not carried by polling or `invalidate-tags` refetches — those are internal caller-less paths. See [docs/lifecycle-hooks.md](docs/lifecycle-hooks.md) for the full pattern.
  - `:on-start` fires with `params` once the query commits to `:loading`/`:fetching?`, before the HTTP effect fires.
  - `:on-success` fires with `params` and post-`:transform-response` data.
  - `:on-failure` fires with `params` and post-`:transform-error` error.

### Changed

- **Internal event shape: `:re-frame.query/query-success` and `:re-frame.query/query-failure` now carry a `hooks` map between `params` and the data/error.** These events are library-internal — users dispatch them only in tests that simulate server responses. If your tests dispatch them directly, add `{}` before the data/error arg: `[:re-frame.query/query-success :books/list {} {} [{:id 1}]]`.

### Docs

- **Renamed `docs/mutation-hooks.md` → `docs/lifecycle-hooks.md`** and expanded to cover query hooks. References in `README.md`, `docs/api-reference.md`, and the skill docs updated.

## [0.7.0] - 2026-04-11

│ changes │
╰─────────╯

Hooks fire only when a fetch actually starts (not on cache hits) and are not carried by polling or `invalidate-tags` refetches — those are internal caller-less paths. See [docs/lifecycle-hooks.md](docs/lifecycle-hooks.md) for the full pattern.
+  - `:on-start` fires with `params` once the query commits to `:loading`/`:fetching?`, before the HTTP effect fires.
+  - `:on-success` fires with `params` and post-`:transform-response` data.
+  - `:on-failure` fires with `params` and post-`:transform-error` error.

## User (2026-04-24 18:35:19)

Honestly the more I look at this, the more I dont' like it. YOur point there that you are better suited just putting an interceptor is actually valid. That is better


## Agent (2026-04-24 18:35:27)

Agreed. Let me trace the implications:

#### What happens if we remove per-call hooks

- **Mutations** already have a strong case for per-call hooks (optimistic updates are keyed by *this specific* mutation's params — `onMutate → onError` context threading is the whole point). Keep those.
- **Queries** don't have the same motivation. The `onSuccess` pattern is almost always: "when this data arrives, do X." That's exactly what an interceptor on `:re-frame.query/query-success` does, with zero library surface area added:

```clojure
(rf/reg-global-interceptor
  (rf/->interceptor
   :id :my-app/on-books-loaded
   :after (fn [context]
            (let [[event-id k params _hooks data] (get-in context [:coeffects :event])]
              (when (and (= event-id :re-frame.query/query-success)
                         (= k :books/list))
                ;; do your thing — toast, analytics, cache patching
                )
              context))))
```

- **Infinite queries** (our next step) also don't need it — same interceptor pattern works on `:re-frame.query/infinite-page-success`.

#### What this simplifies

1. Drop `:on-start` / `:on-success` / `:on-failure` from `ensure-query`, `refetch-query`, and the `::rfq/query` subscription.
2. Delete the `hooks` arg threading through `build-query-effects`, `query-success`, `query-failure`.
3. Revert the `query-success` / `query-failure` event shape back to `[k params data]` / `[k params error]` — removes the 40+ test-dispatch edits (which were a code smell, honestly).
4. Delete the new query-hook tests — the interceptor pattern is tested by the consuming app, not the library.
5. Doc turns into a recipe rather than an API.

#### What we'd actually write

A section in `docs/lifecycle-hooks.md` titled something like **"Observing query lifecycle via interceptors"** with:
- The `reg-global-interceptor` pattern above
- How to filter by event-id + query key
- Mention that `:re-frame.query/query-success` carries `[k params data]`, etc. — i.e., document the events as a public observation surface even though they're "internal" for dispatching
- A note that if you need something fancier (e.g. different behavior per call site), either use a mutation (which has per-call hooks) or dispatch your own event after `ensure-query` and use the interceptor to tag it

#### What about `on-start`?

`on-start` was the weakest of the three anyway — it fires after `:loading`/`:fetching?` is committed. An interceptor on `:re-frame.query/ensure-query` after-handler can see the same transition. Or users can dispatch their "start" event themselves right before `ensure-query`:

```clojure
(rf/dispatch [:analytics/books-fetch-started])
(rf/dispatch [::rfq/ensure-query :books/list {}])
```

That's arguably clearer than a hook anyway.

## My recommendation

**Fully revert query hooks.** Keep mutation hooks as-is (they earn their complexity). Add a "Observing queries via interceptors" section to the docs.

The one thing I'd flag: we already committed and landed the staged changes. Reverting means another few commits undoing work. That's fine — the feature isn't released (still unreleased in CHANGELOG), so the cost is just commit noise on `main`, not user-visible breakage.

Want me to proceed with the revert?

## User (2026-04-24 18:37:58)

Is there a way to register a global interceptor only on a particular route and then uninstall it on leave? for example? Just to not pollute the global event queue 


## Agent (2026-04-24 18:38:01)

Yes — `reg-global-interceptor` returns the interceptor and there's `clear-global-interceptor` that takes the `:id`. So route enter/leave can install/uninstall cleanly.

980	(defn clear-global-interceptor
981	  "Unregisters global interceptors (presumably registered previously via the use of `reg-global-interceptor`).
982	
983	  When called with no args, it will unregister all currently registered global interceptors.
984	
985	  When given one arg, assumed to be the `id` of a previously registered
986	  global interceptors, it will unregister the associated interceptor. Will produce a warning to
987	  console if it finds no matching registration."
988	  {:api-docs/heading "Global Interceptors"}
989	  ([]
990	   (settings/clear-global-interceptors))
991	  ([id]
992	   (settings/clear-global-interceptors id)))
993
